impacts.category: GHAS Seats Kusto Data Mob Session with Dorothy Pearce, Ashwin Mohan, Aakash Shah

Mike identified that the fundamental problem was not the queries themselves but the lack of organizational alignment: "I don't even care if the query's imperfect, as long as it's close enough. The only thing I care about is that we're all aligned on which query we're using."

impacts.category: Big Orca OKR Chat with Dorothy Pearce, Steve Richert, Tara Kintner, Nic Daantos, Aakash Shah

impacts.category: Growth RTB and Board Walkthrough with Dorothy Pearce, Ashwin Mohan
impacts.description: Mike contributed substantively to the metrics discussion, proposing a unified 50% threshold for both code security and secret protection to simplify leadership communication. Mike also challenged the team's adoption metrics direction, questioning whether speed-to-adoption is the right metric when median time is already 0 days, and proposed reframing around increasing the number of adopters rather than accelerating existing ones.

Mike driving initiative to establish reliable seats/churn metrics. feedback.description: On his last week, Chanakya prioritized getting the trial extension feature flag removal PR into a reviewable state (27 files, ~1500 lines) so whoever picks it up has full context. He also caught a small bug in the process. Solid handoff work on the way out.

Participated in the Incident #4131 response as part of the Security Products Enablement team, contributing to root cause identification within ~20 minutes during the incident call.

Confirmed familiarity with the mismatched configs process when asked, but was mostly quiet during technical discussions. Taking over FR sync facilitation for the next two weeks while Nic is out, which will be a good opportunity to step up.

Keeps getting assigned work that shrinks in scope (GHAS billing being the latest example). Not a performance issue on his part, but a recurring frustration that needs active management attention to ensure he gets appropriately challenging work.

Proactively explored Figma MCP integration on his own, successfully pulling CSRA designs into chat. Identified the tooling gap (dev entitlements needed) and surfaced it as a team opportunity.

Wrote a thoughtful discussion post proposing how the team should adapt its workflow for agentic development. Mike read it twice, discussed it with AI, and found the ideas largely non-controversial and well-reasoned. Chanakya articulated the shift from code-as-bottleneck to coordination-as-bottleneck clearly. # Feedback Log _Continuous feedback tracking for performance conversations._

👍 2025-10-28 — positive Chanakya demonstrated strong knowledge of the billing infrastructure during the data discussion. He identified the root cause of the trial end date bug (subscription item records being overwritten on conversion) and articulated the technical constraints clearly to Mike and Dorothy. Need to think thoroughly about the purpose of the work you're doing to understand what makes sense and what doesn't.

**Context:** Team retrospective Most engaged contribution to a team meeting in a while. Wrote the "card of the retro" about code quality and stricter reviews. Gave specific examples (1,077-line security config model, inherited SRA style). Demonstrated using AI to generate concrete refactoring suggestions in PR reviews instead of vague comments. Volunteered to create copilot-instructions.md in the monolith. Also mentioned starting refactoring work proactively. This is the kind of ownership and initiative we need to see more of.

Only shows when a mismatch exists between billing model and configuration. Directly helps support teams. Good example of identifying and solving a practical operational problem without being asked.

Epic grew in scope unexpectedly, requiring trial architecture overhaul. ~6 weeks of planning/ADR with no code output. Quality misses occurred. Afraid of looking dumb, doesn't want to distract in a 30-minute meeting. Acknowledged "I've been on this team too long to feel that way." Committed to being more vocal in code reviews and meetings.

This is exactly the kind of force-multiplier work that has outsized impact.

**Context:** CSRA enablement work / PR review discussion before Mike's 1:1 with Chanakya Jennifer said Chanakya is doing great work overall, but flagged a specific execution pattern on recent CSRA enablement work: he picked up one issue (#2607) but implemented a different one (#2625), wired the enable link to the wrong dialog, and combined that work with a large refactor that blocked shipping a feature needed to unblock enablement. Her view was that the refactor itself was valid, but a refactor of that size should have been a dedicated PR, with the feature unblocked first via a smaller targeted change.

**Context:** 1:1 discussion of Autopilot and recent work Chanakya showed strong product judgment by narrowing Autopilot's scope to a smaller initial audience and a more concrete capability, focusing on Copilot users and PR chaining instead of trying to solve every provider/workflow at once. He also connected the project clearly to real developer pain and internal adoption paths.

**Context:** 1:1 discussion of team process and code quality Chanakya articulated thoughtful feedback about how the team's bottlenecks have shifted. He pointed out that writing code is no longer the slow part, review/CI/deploy are, and suggested scoping work into more coherent, independently-owned streams. He also tied maintainability concerns back to concrete examples in the CSRA/SRA package rather than making vague complaints.

**Context:** 1:1 discussion of recent CSRA execution confusion Chanakya acknowledged that he picked up related CSRA work without enough shared context or explicit ownership, which contributed to confusion about which issue was being implemented and a UI flow being wired to the wrong dialog. The takeaway is not "don't refactor," it is to sync on context/ownership first and optimize for the right thing when there is demo or unblock pressure.

**Context:** [GitHub discussion post](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/discussions/2694) — "Rethinking how we execute feature work" Chanakya published a thoughtful discussion post arguing that as agent-assisted coding speeds up implementation, the bottleneck shifts from writing code to coordination, context recovery, and sequencing. He proposed letting one person own a coherent feature end-to-end using parent/child issue graphs with stacked PRs, rather than splitting features across multiple people. The post showed strong systems thinking about how the team works and a willingness to push the conversation forward publicly.

Self-identified the need for an ADR on enabling self-serve trials for Team plan orgs and wrote it up with a quick turnaround (PR #2765). Showed initiative by spotting the gap in trial consistency across plan types and moving to close it without being asked.
Completing CSRA CSV download work and making progress on multiple first responder PRs simultaneously, including the data retention table cleanup PR.

Proactively proposed writing an ADR for self-serve Team plan trials after discussing conversion data with Dorothy. Identified that CSRA shows ~4% conversion for Team plans (comparable to GHEC), challenging the assumption that trials wouldn't be worth it. Came with a rough estimate of ~2 iterations for 1-2 people.

Fixed a tricky bundled/unbundled security config bug where rebundling set fields to false instead of nil, causing unbundling to incorrectly remove configs. Fixed the root cause and added a workaround for existing bad data, showing thoroughness in handling both the fix and legacy state.

Raised the important concern that redefining existing enforcement would retroactively change behavior for enterprise admins who didn't opt into it. Also identified that the filter selection bug could reuse the pattern she already implemented in CSRA.
Completed the entire CSRA epic and checked off every single checkbox on the tracking issue. Mike noted this might be the first time every checkbox was completed.

Identified a systemic issue from the CSRA project: the ~1700-line front-end file from secret risk assessment caused friction for Chanakya and Nic during development. Proposed a reusable solution (pre-project refactoring) rather than just complaining about it. Working through banner cleanup with Catu to pay down the tech debt now.

Only person on the team who actually used the epic planning checklist as intended, going through and checking off items. Then used that experience to propose concrete improvements: splitting the checklist into phased issues and adding a code preparation step during planning.

Proactively investigated and fixed two CSRA post-launch bugs (500 errors on large orgs, assessment failure retry issue). Had a PR up for the batching fix before anyone asked. Reached out to Anders on the code scanning team independently to diagnose the assessment failure.

On track to ship download CSV this week. Dorothy confirmed Jennifer messaged her with the update, and Katu collaborated with her on the design. Shows good cross-functional communication and delivery momentum.

Already has the download CSV endpoint PR up and expects to ship no later than tomorrow. Proactively raised the question about GHES edge cases (code scanning disabled) and suggested following the secret risk assessment pattern for consistency.

Asked clarifying questions about 422 error ownership and proposed a practical solution (periodic reconciliation job) that aligned with the direction Tara was already leaning. Volunteered to take FR rotation with no concerns about CSRA conflicts.

Called out important context at the start of the stack undo debrief that Ayman was confused about which interview he was doing and the time allotment. Provided clear technical observations, noting Ayman's Python fluency was fine but over-designing was the problem. For Siman, highlighted specific positive signals like him immediately identifying correct data structures and proactively calling out limitations in his own design choices.
Showed strong ownership instinct by flagging concern that the temporary fix could mask a deeper architectural issue, rather than just shipping the quick fix and moving on.

Proactively brought the CSRA name collision bug to the 1:1 before making a decision, walked through the root cause clearly, and already had a working PR ready. Also consulted Steve for a second opinion before escalating.

Dorothy reported that after investigating the CSRA enablement bug, she and Jennifer now feel good about the state of things. Jennifer is owning the fix and managing multiple CSRA bugs while also doing interviews.

Confirmed the scheduler timeout fix is now working in production, closing out a previous reliability concern. Also proactively took ownership of investigating the enablement button's intended vs. actual behavior to resolve confusion found during testing.
Proactively asked about getting more complex, ADR-level work, showing awareness of growth areas and initiative in seeking stretch opportunities.
Hit the ground running after returning from PTO. Opened the final quick setup volume PR on his first day back. Multiple PRs and code review activity visible since returning.
First day back from trip and already oriented on the volume licensing UX work. Asked smart clarifying question about feature flag gating and confirmed approach with Tara.
Mike identified billing budgets as a good fit for Nic, noting it would be good for his promo packet. Suggests confidence in Nic's ability to own a feature end-to-end.

Participated in the Incident #4131 response as part of the Security Products Enablement team, helping trace root cause from 500 errors to the collab primary to the specific query and job within ~20 minutes.
Participated in the Incident #4131 response as part of the Security Products Enablement team, contributing to root cause identification within ~20 minutes during the incident call.

Shipped the mismatched configs transition PR using a solid hybrid approach (primaries for direct writes, wrapped downstream calls). Also handled the Wayfair incident transition quickly and made a good suggestion to close the Wayfair transition issue as done rather than rescoping it, since the transition work was complete.

Self-identified that investigation skills need more development. Acknowledged being slower on the 422 issue and having difficulty piecing together information. Making progress but still a growth area.
Went through multiple iterations with Steve Richert on the Wayfair transition and got approval. Proactively planned a dry run to validate numbers against Mike's Kusto query before executing.
After running the wrong job, stayed calm, assessed the damage, and reported it transparently. Contrasted this with how he would have panicked at a previous employer, crediting the team culture.
Second FR rotation showing clear improvement. Self-assessed that he now has 'a fighting chance' reading escalations and customer issues, compared to having no idea during his first rotation.

**Context:** Meeting on 2026-03-24 > > During the incident response call for the collab cluster availability impact (incident #41), Steve demonstrated strong ownership of the `apply_security_configuration_to_repository` job. He clearly stated "we squarely own this job" (17:05:23) and immediately started asking targeted diagnostic questions: whether the query was hitting primary vs. replica, whether execution time had degraded, and what the actual throughput looked like. He proposed a practical short-term mitigation (moving the read off primary onto a replica) and agreed with the throttling approach suggested by others. He then volunteered his team to take the offline action item of locating the query in code and ensuring it runs against replicas (17:26:32). He also stayed on the call digging through code in real time and collaborated with Matt from Licensing to trace the query origin. > Steve acknowledged he "kind of joined the party a little late" (17:04:20) to an incident caused by a job his team squarely owns. By the time he joined, others (Chris Kirkland, Blake Williams, Xin Wu) had already identified the problematic query, traced it to Adobe's business ID, and discussed throttling. His initial questions ("What is the shape of what we're seeing?" and "I can't reconcile those yet") suggest he wasn't monitoring the incident channel before joining the call. For a job the team owns, earlier engagement in the incident response would have been helpful. > Chanakya was not present on the incident call. Steve mentioned Chanakya by name at the end (17:28:34) when organizing a debrief, suggesting Chanakya is part of the team that owns this job. Worth checking whether Chanakya was aware of the incident and whether there's a gap in on-call coverage or incident alerting for the team. > Similar to Chanakya, Nic was referenced by Steve at the end of the call (17:28:34) as someone to debrief with, but did not appear to be on the incident call itself. Same question applies about awareness and coverage.

**Context:** Meeting on 2026-03-19 > > Tara had multiple PRs (trial banners, bundle metered for quick setup) approved and ready to merge before their time off. They prepared a detailed handoff document using a GHE gist, including a bug bash section with edge cases sketched out (e.g., what happens when both paid products are disabled, license count behavior). They committed to fixing broken enterprise tests before end of day. > During the discussion about reapplying failed security configs, Tara flagged a significant customer-impact risk: that some of these accounts might be in a weird state due to trial lifecycle interactions (trial ends, org upgrades to enterprise), and reapplying could result in surprise billing. This pushed the conversation toward the safer approach of marking them as failed instead. Good instinct for protecting customer trust over taking the expedient fix. > When Mike raised the concern about reapplying configs across potentially 500k repos, Tara immediately suggested a practical mitigation: batching the transition and paging through it in chunks rather than doing it all at once. > Nic proactively reached out to Tara (via Slack) to get context on whether failed security configs should be reapplied, pulling in domain expertise before taking action. This triggered the broader discussion about the right remediation approach.

Pushed back constructively on the enterprise enforcement proposal, arguing they should expand options rather than redefine existing behavior. Proposed a backwards-compatible approach with tiered enforcement levels. Also caught a potential risk in the zero-seat conversion plan by noting zero seats could be a temporary state.

Delivered a thorough, well-structured ADR walkthrough covering the full planner/stager/attacher architecture. Proactively addressed failure modes (sweepers, stuck work recovery, Hydro message loss), considered alternatives for each design decision, and documented them clearly. Incorporated Aakash's config versioning feedback before presenting.

Identified a meaningful gap in the GHAS bottom-up adoption proposal: if work stream 1 only covers individually owned private repos, free org-owned repos become the only repo type that can't have GHAS. Dorothy called it a "very helpful comment" and "really good point." Also pushed constructively on work stream 3 by questioning the developer use case and pointing out that active committer pricing doesn't correlate with actual scan cost (which is driven by repo size).

Proactively decided to stop blocking on Chanakya and the org ADR review for the attachment pipeline work. Plans to break it into issues and milestones independently, showing good initiative on moving the project forward.

Self-identified recurring pattern: drops the ball on communication, visibility, and Slack engagement. Described these as "never novel" feedback areas. Does not use GitHub notifications on desktop and rarely monitors Slack channels, causing him to miss team PRs and discussions.

PR review volume was 12 in March against a ~25/month target. Team members consistently call out his reviews as valuable (mentioned in the last retro), but he's only reviewing PRs assigned via rotation rather than proactively scanning team PRs. As staff engineer, the expectation is broader coverage.

Thoughtfully coordinating the attachment pipeline ADR, wanting to bring it through the team first before the org review. Asked a good question about enabling feature flags in GHES for testing. Also raised where the pipeline work fits on the roadmap to get clarity.

Provided the most detailed and structured feedback of any interviewer in the Siman/Ayman debrief. Went dimension-by-dimension through the rubric, cited specific candidate behaviors (defending vs incorporating feedback, stress accumulation, naming conventions, class design decisions), and had written notes ready for Siman. Submitted feedback to iSIMs promptly. Also proactively flagged a candidate experience issue around the reschedule communication as a constructive data point.

Participated in the Incident #4131 response call and helped trace root cause within ~20 minutes. His attachment pipeline work (ADR in progress) is identified as the longer-term architectural fix that would eliminate the mass fan-out spike pattern entirely.

Participated in the Incident #4131 response as part of the Security Products Enablement team, helping trace root cause within ~20 minutes. Three immediate repair items are already done. His longer-term attachment pipeline work (ADR in progress) addresses the underlying mass fan-out pattern that caused the incident.

Steve proactively sent unsolicited positive feedback about a teammate (Chanakya) via Slack DM, recognizing Chanakya's AI expertise and knowledge-sharing contributions. Shows awareness of team dynamics and willingness to advocate for peers.

Raised a critical question about whether the root cause of mismatched configs was actually fixed before running the cleanup transition: 'a cleanup is great, but it's even better if we're cleaning up a mess that's not still being made.' Also provided strong technical pushback on the 422 monitoring strategy, distinguishing between expected user-error 422s and exceptional ones the UI should prevent, which reshaped the team's approach to the issue.
Self-identified that sharing work broadly is a 'weak muscle.' Asked Mike to send him specific Slack channels, which signals awareness but also a need to build this habit independently.

Biggest gap right now is visibility at the senior director level (Aaron Cathcart). Staff-level calibration happens above Mike's level, and Steve's work isn't reaching that audience. Needs to actively push his work into more visible channels: security products Slack, org-wide ADR review, direct touchpoints with Aaron and Ashwin.

Showed good judgment holding off on slicing the attachment pipeline ADR into milestones, proactively coordinating with Chanakya to see if the ADR could serve as a test bed for a new way of organizing work.
Has been giving thorough, instructive code reviews to Nic on the transition PR and use_primaries work. Also took time to walk Mike through how use_connection_role_reading works.

Responded to constructive feedback from ~2 weeks ago by completing two key deliverables: fixed the uptime SLO and shipped the attachment pipeline ADR. Both were the specific asks from that feedback conversation.

**Context:** Meeting on 2026-03-24 > > During the incident response call for the collab cluster availability impact (incident #41), Steve demonstrated strong ownership of the `apply_security_configuration_to_repository` job. He clearly stated "we squarely own this job" (17:05:23) and immediately started asking targeted diagnostic questions: whether the query was hitting primary vs. replica, whether execution time had degraded, and what the actual throughput looked like. He proposed a practical short-term mitigation (moving the read off primary onto a replica) and agreed with the throttling approach suggested by others. He then volunteered his team to take the offline action item of locating the query in code and ensuring it runs against replicas (17:26:32). He also stayed on the call digging through code in real time and collaborated with Matt from Licensing to trace the query origin. > Steve acknowledged he "kind of joined the party a little late" (17:04:20) to an incident caused by a job his team squarely owns. By the time he joined, others (Chris Kirkland, Blake Williams, Xin Wu) had already identified the problematic query, traced it to Adobe's business ID, and discussed throttling. His initial questions ("What is the shape of what we're seeing?" and "I can't reconcile those yet") suggest he wasn't monitoring the incident channel before joining the call. For a job the team owns, earlier engagement in the incident response would have been helpful. > Chanakya was not present on the incident call. Steve mentioned Chanakya by name at the end (17:28:34) when organizing a debrief, suggesting Chanakya is part of the team that owns this job. Worth checking whether Chanakya was aware of the incident and whether there's a gap in on-call coverage or incident alerting for the team. > Similar to Chanakya, Nic was referenced by Steve at the end of the call (17:28:34) as someone to debrief with, but did not appear to be on the incident call itself. Same question applies about awareness and coverage.

Great code review for Ashwin's "Allow repo admins to modify delegated bypass settings when security config is not enforced" PR here https://github.com/github/github/pull/423996#pullrequestreview-4001025803

Described reaching a sustainable relationship with AI tooling after months of adjustment. No longer "performance maxing" and using Copilot effectively in VS Code for tasks like data shape verification, while still maintaining code understanding.

Proactively approved Nic's first volume pricing PR and is tracking the flow's progress across pages. Also posted in a channel to check for prior art on unified validation before starting, showing good due diligence.

Opened a refactor PR for the quick setup feature guide that extracts scattered business logic into three clean pattern types (auto-enabled, elective, required core). This simplifies adding new features from modifying multiple functions down to adding a single item to a structure. Used Copilot effectively with detailed prompts to produce the refactor.

HPC performance dashboard shows major improvement after their optimization work. Also fixed two intermittent test flakes and shipped a fix for a missing OpenAPI required parameter, using Copilot SRE to help with root cause analysis.

Proactively identified that issues #2260 and #2601 should be deferred until the attachment pipeline ships, and proposed creating a parent issue to track post-pipeline work. Steve called it a good idea since it could inform pipeline design decisions.

Showed strong product instinct by pushing back on the silent coercion approach (discussed at FR sync), raising the concern that it would mean the UI is lying to users. Advocated for the harder but more correct solution.

Identified a deep architectural root cause for recurring 422 errors: UI and Ruby model validations are maintained separately across two repos with no linkage. Rather than proposing a quick patch, they proposed a unified validator class that serializes rules for both backend and frontend, eliminating the sync problem. Estimated the work at ~2 iterations and volunteered to own it.

Proactively investigated and opened a PR to fix a performance regression on the Enterprise config page (P2 web vitals issue from FR board). The regression roughly doubled response times for the common case. Tara traced it back to the specific PR that caused it using monitoring charts and shipped a fix as a side quest during FR rotation.

Asked sharp questions during the ADR walkthrough: immediately asked about the public interface ("is it a Hydro event? a method call?"), raised the complexity concern around Kafka partitioning and hot partitions, and pushed on practical UI implications for enterprise org admins waiting in queue. Helped ground the discussion in real-world impact.
Raised good clarifying questions about whether metrics gaps require new data collection vs. query changes, helping scope the follow-up work with Dorothy.

Led the design discussion effectively, pushing back thoughtfully on duplicating warning messages across all three screens and articulating clear UX rationale. Also reported volume pricing is behind a feature flag and functionally working, with only UI implementation remaining.

Made progress on volume billing behind a feature flag, fixed a quick setup bug, and is actively collaborating with Katu on designs for license information display. Also proactively suggested testing in a GHES environment before scheduling the product call.

Engaging thoughtfully with the new performance framework, planning to structure both her self-reflection and peer reviews around the four dimensions (craft, direction, people, impact) rather than just following default prompts.

Proactively thinking about Chanakya's departure risks, specifically flagging the danger of shipping the feature flag removal too close to his last day. Suggested a Monday cutoff, showing good operational judgment.

Drove quick setup volume pricing to a near-shippable state, merging multiple PRs last week. Feature flag is up and working. Has a clear plan for the remaining work (designs from Cat, 422 investigation, GAS disabled edge case) with a realistic May 1st target.

Proactively investigated 422 errors by pulling Splunk logs and using Copilot to group ~50 errors into categories (issue 2741). Identified the root cause as a brittle validation architecture and articulated the tradeoffs clearly. Also caught and fixed a log noise issue from a null reference she spotted in logs.

Caught a latent performance issue in PR #426240 where a query would trigger 2 full table scans at scale (>50K rows). Didn't just flag the problem, she proposed a concrete fix with indexes on created_at and owner_id plus cursor-based batching. This is the kind of forward-looking review that prevents production incidents.
A quick setup bug was found in production related to default configuration label display when switching between configs. Mike noted this scenario likely wasn't tested during Tara's quick setup work.

**Context:** Meeting on 2026-03-19 > > Tara had multiple PRs (trial banners, bundle metered for quick setup) approved and ready to merge before their time off. They prepared a detailed handoff document using a GHE gist, including a bug bash section with edge cases sketched out (e.g., what happens when both paid products are disabled, license count behavior). This pushed the conversation toward the safer approach of marking them as failed instead. Good instinct for protecting customer trust over taking the expedient fix. > When Mike raised the concern about reapplying configs across potentially 500k repos, Tara immediately suggested a practical mitigation: batching the transition and paging through it in chunks rather than doing it all at once. > Nic proactively reached out to Tara (via Slack) to get context on whether failed security configs should be reapplied, pulling in domain expertise before taking action. reviews.content:
# FY26 H1 Review: Chanakya's

_Manager Reflection, FY26 Reflection_
_Period: June - December 2025 (FY26 H1)_

---

## Question 1: What results did you deliver, and how did you do it?

### Manager Response 

Chanakya started the cycle by ramping up on the Enterprise Security Manager project. This was his second straight stint embedding into a separate team (the first was Spark), so he was once again in a position of navigating an unfamiliar codebase and a team of people he didn't know. He spent about three months working on this project and in that time he rolled out the ESM role across all surfaces, which was critical blocking work for the staff ship. He shipped a set of PRs that unified what had been a fragmented experience, and built it on top of shared components to make it maintainable and consistent.

**Peer feedback:**

> "He jumped in quickly to our new React code and operated independently to start delivery quickly and consistently. He needed a bit more support on backend PRs through PR review, but was able to accept feedback and iterate effectively."

When he finally was able to come back to the team, he jumped into the pricing calculator work. He quickly had solid throughput across frontend and backend and also shipped fixes for a lot of the issues that we flagged in the bug bash.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Chanakya leaned into AI as a force multiplier at the end of the secret risk assessment project and rapidly closed a significant number of bug issues, despite having just come off another project with limited context. His willingness to experiment, learn tooling quickly, and apply it pragmatically turned what could have been a slow re-ramp into immediate momentum."

His most significant project this cycle was leading the work of extending trial eligibility and making the call to consolidate self-serve trials under the SKUTrial architecture rather than bolting onto the legacy Billing system. He quickly rallied around this idea when Aakash/Jennifer brought it up in the initial ADR review, and showed a lot of flexibility and energy around it rather than staying attached to his original plan. His next ADR was complete and detailed: it laid out migration phases, multi-trial limits, cooldown logic, and rollback strategy. This work is still in progress, but it will extend eligibility to many thousands more enterprises, and it will make the overall trial architecture much more maintainable and consistent.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Chanakya has exhibited strong leadership in his work to extend eligibility for self-serve GHAS trials. Chanakya always makes a great DRI because of the thoroughness of his thinking and planning."

I also want to call out Chanakya's championing of Copilot throughout the cycle since coming back to the team. During his FR shift in November, Chanakya used Copilot to rapidly close out a wave of accessibility fixes and Sentry exceptions. He also notably used Copilot in researching and writing the ADR(s) for extending eligibility, after experimenting with using Copilot to experiment with going straight to a POC PR for discussion instead of an ADR. Those are bigger examples, but there are countless smaller examples of Chanakya using Copilot to make everyday tasks easier. In a recent mob session, he even encouraged Steve to try using Copilot to answer a question instead of going digging in the code. He is a great example for the rest of the team, giving them inspiration and ideas for how to make use of AI in their daily work, and testing its limits so that the rest of the team can benefit from seeing what works and what doesn't without having to hit those walls themselves. He's been generous about sharing his workflow with teammates.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Using AI to accelerate small fixes and boilerplate let him focus attention on the actual behaviors we needed to stabilize. That combination of fast ramp + productive use of AI is a differentiating strength, please keep pairing experimentation with quick delivery like this."

> "Chanakya's repeatable rapid ramp, rejoining a project with minimal context and becoming productive almost immediately, is a standout strength. Pairing that adaptability with his effective AI usage gives the team real throughput gains."

> "I hope Chanakya keeps pushing those boundaries and challenging our entrenched processes so we can keep finding new efficiencies."

He also served as Nic's onboarding buddy throughout this period, often being the first to answer questions and building a solid relationship from the start, which made a huge difference in Nic's onboarding experience. Nic was productive fairly quickly after joining and I credit a lot of that to Chanakya's support and mentoring.

**Peer feedback (from Nic):**

> "I think Chanakya's Growth Mindset leads him to ask the most questions compared to anyone else on the team. I personally find this beneficial because most of questions he asks are questions that I didn't even know I had, so the resulting discussions are helpful to myself and hopefully the rest of the team."

> "I love working with Chanakya, and all the positivity he brings to the team!"

### Employee Response

**Enterprise Security Manager (ESM) Capability Rollout**

Executed a multi-step rollout of the new ESM role across enterprise, org, team, and user assignment surfaces, introducing validation, permission visualization, consistency in styling, and guardrails (redirects when limits reached; enforcement of assignment constraints; surfacing delegated metadata). This converted a fragmented experience into a coherent role management pathway for large enterprises evaluating advanced security.

Key results:
- Delivered a progressive sequence of role assignment enhancements (enterprise, org, team, user) under feature flags, enabling safe incremental adoption
- Reduced cognitive load via shared componentization (role permissions rendering now unified rather than copy/paste divergence), simplifying future additions of security roles
- Added stricter validation + redirect logic, decreasing user frustration at hidden limit states

Representative merged PRs (ESM surface & UX): #379905 #381151 #382675 #384799 #385452 #386009 #388972 #392671 #395819 #396326

**Secret Scanning / Pricing Calculator & Selective Enablement**

Implemented the selective repository enablement backend endpoint and then iteratively refined UI flows: repository filtering providers, PATCH semantics, accurate cost breakdown states for "selected repositories", responsive dialog layout, success feedback, and archived repo differentiation. This lowered friction for security teams estimating and enabling GHAS components in a controlled manner.

Impact highlights:
- Introduced granular, selective enablement flow, shifting from all-or-nothing toggles to targeted repository activation
- Improved correctness (cost breakdown zero states vs full enrollment) and clarity (distinct success banner, archived repo visual differentiation)
- Elevated maintainability via refactoring inline styles to structured modules and centralizing mutation logic

Representative merged PRs: Backend: #397984 #398481 / UI: github-ui #985 #1284 #1294 #1399 #1401 #1405 #1412

**GHAS Trial Modernization & Strategic Expansion**

Contributed materially to expanding standalone GHAS trial eligibility and to the architectural decision to unify self-serve trials under the SKUTrial system. Work on issue #1631 clarified user segments, hypotheses, and risk posture; the ADR codifies migration phases, multi-trial limits, cooldown, trial count/end-date tracking, and rollback strategy. Outcome: positioned Growth to iterate independently of Billing, enabling future multi-trial experiments and richer conversion analytics.

**Accessibility & Quality (Copilot-Assisted)**

Leveraging Copilot, triaged and merged a concentrated wave of accessibility and UX correctness fixes (semantic headings, keyboard focus order, aria-current for tab states, color contrast, focus return in dialogs, resilient null-safe local storage, defensive guards against undefined DOM targets). Transformed latent audit items into shipped improvements rapidly while preserving developer velocity.

Results:
- Resolved multiple WCAG violations (1.4.1 Use of Color, 4.1.2 Name/Role/Value, focus order)
- Elevated keyboard and screen reader experience for security configuration & risk assessment interfaces
- Hardened UI error surfaces (TypeError prevention in actions-policy form & CopilotLocalStorage)

Representative merged Copilot-authored/assisted PRs: Accessibility & focus: github #400568, github-ui #1958 #1957 #1956 #1868 / Resilience/quality fixes: github #399642 #399541, github-ui #2379 #2382

**Operational Integrity & Security Posture**

- Executed disablement cleanup (mis-enabled code security) to correct unintended repository states (#400066), reducing unnecessary attack surface & billing confusion
- Configured ghas_trial background processing for proxima (#399541), improving throughput and latency for trial eligibility and expiration jobs
- Stabilized flaky tests by replacing hardcoded IDs with deterministic non-existent IDs (#399642), restoring trust in CI signal for security center logic

**Early Copilot UX (Transition Phase)**

Before fully pivoting to ESM/security work, improved Copilot's logged-out immersive experience (model selection persistence, correct auto-submit sequencing, modal orchestration) enabling more reliable onboarding for prospective Copilot users. Representative merged PRs: #375971 #377249

**Leveraging AI (Copilot)**

1. As an accelerator for routine but high-impact accessibility & reliability fixes, reviewing, validating, and contextualizing Copilot-authored PRs to clear a backlog rapidly. Reduced mean time to remediation for audit findings from "multi-week" to "days" in September.
2. Pairing with Copilot inline suggestions to refactor repetitive UI logic (repository selection flows, role permissions component reuse), increasing consistency and lowering cognitive overhead.
3. Used Copilot for extensive code explorations to come up with idea map for existing subscription flow architecture for GHAS trials, write a clear ADR and create issues.

**Security Contributions**

- Introduced and hardened the ESM role across hierarchical surfaces, improving least-privilege assignment clarity and reducing misconfiguration risk
- Delivered selective secret scanning enablement endpoints & UI, supporting incremental adoption paths instead of blunt global toggles
- Executed cleanup to reverse unintended security feature enablement to align actual usage with intended authorization state
- Expanded GHAS trial eligibility safely (cooldown, seat limits, multi-trial rules) to grow security feature evaluation while embedding anti-abuse safeguards
- Improved accessibility of security configuration experiences, making critical security controls more usable for all customers

---

## Question 2: Reflect on recent challenges

### Manager Response

Chanakya's answer here shows solid self-awareness. The trial eligibility work was a good learning experience because it didn't go smoothly at first. The core issue was jumping to implementation before alignment, but the theory behind it was that the Copilot-assisted PR could serve as alignment. That was a worthy experiment to run. So he spiked a PR against the Billing system instead of writing and getting alignment on an ADR, and when Billing review brought up some surprises, he had to pivot. That said, he took the feedback well, wrote and shared an ADR, and spun out issues for the team.

**Peer feedback:**

> "As Chanakya prepares for senior responsibilities, the key focus area is leading with more visible architectural and process ownership, especially documenting rationale before implementation. In the recent project he initially used AI to jump straight to a PR instead of first writing an ADR, which was unexpected and led to a pivot for broader alignment across GHAS trials. Importantly, he took that feedback, produced a proper ADR, and spun out issues for the team, great corrective action. The senior-level shift now is to make that sequence (ADR, team alignment, implementation) the default, not reactive."

> "He tried something new (for our team) in co-writing the trial consolidation ADR with Copilot. He used Copilot's authorship as a starting point and at one point had gone further, just asking Copilot to author the finished product to see how far it could go without any hand-holding. Ultimately, I think Chanakya successfully found the limit of the current usefulness of that approach, which I believe was the purpose."

**Manager's November feedback (for posterity):**

> "Wondering if the extending-trial-eligibility could have benefitted from a more standard epic planning process, with an ADR and team walkthrough and creating issues before diving into the work? I get a little bit of a 'building the airplane while it's flying' vibe from it this week, in that a good chunk of it is in review while another key piece is an open question, and people are being asked to review work that they don't have context on yet like they would if they had read/discussed the ADR."

**Manager's additional feedback:**

We rate performance only on impact, and at the SE3 level impact comes from shipping, reviewing, and accelerating others. Relative to that expectation, Chanakya's impact so far this cycle has been slightly lower than expected.

We talked during the ESM embed about the slowdown after joining that team. His explanation for the dip was reasonable (unfamiliar codebase, test infra overhead, Primer/CSS work), and his output picked up quickly once he returned to the home team. Then, more recently, there was a stretch of ~7 weeks with no shipped work while planning the epic for extending trial eligibility. Even accounting for the architectural shift toward SKUTrial and the ADR rewrites, this planning period ran longer than expected and further limited his overall impact for the cycle.

What matters now is whether this period reflects a temporary speedbump or a trend. If it's a speedbump, and he returns to his usual pace of shipping, reviewing, and enabling others over the rest of the cycle, the trajectory for the rest of the cycle can still be strong. If the slowdown continues, the overall impact for the cycle will likely fall short of SE3 expectations.

Outside of that, there is also a visibility gap between Chanakya's work and his teammates. Chanakya does good work and has a lot of knowledge, but he doesn't tend to share progress, context, or decisions in real time with his team as often as I (and they) would like. As a result, teammates often don't know what he's working on or what expertise they could be leveraging, which means they miss opportunities to collaborate or ask for help. This is important for both the home team when not embedding and the "away" team when embedding.

**Peer feedback:**

> "I think I've had the least visibility over what Chanakya is working on on a day-to-day basis. While it's clear he's a talented engineer with plenty of strengths, I don't always have a sense of his specific expertise areas. This sometimes means I might miss opportunities to reach out for help or collaborate, simply because I don't know when Chanakya is the right person to ask. Maybe an improvement to this work visibility could help the team know when to lean on him for advice."

> "I'd love to see Chanakya do more teaching. For example, can we talk about experimenting with (or even eventually formalizing) a Copilot-first ADR process? What would that look like? I don't really know, but it seems like Chanakya might. Some of Chanakya's skills, especially around AI, probably seem commonplace to him, but feel like sorcery to me."

> (Re: the ESM embed) "He kept to himself during the project, only focusing on his own issues. Especially in a cross-team/cross-org project like this, I would have loved to see him more proactively share his expertise, particularly in the security products domain which my team is less familiar with. For example, my teammate was quite vocal she needed help with setting up alerts for scale testing and validating security features, and I feel like that was a missed opportunity for collaboration from Chanakya, but I also would have loved to see him participate more in discussions or PR review. I know ESM wasn't his normal team, but I'd encourage him to find balance between strong IC contributions, and integrating and engaging with the team in ways outside his immediate deliverables."

This connects to feedback from earlier in the cycle. In May, Belinda reached out about responsiveness on Slack and attendance at team calls. The feedback at the time: "Stay responsive to Belinda and the team while they build trust in you and vice versa. Referring to being late or missing team calls or responding quickly to Slack threads. She DM'ed me last week about this stuff so it'd be good to kind of overdo it for a little while." This feedback focused more on responsiveness than visibility, which is not an issue since coming back to the team, but it fits within the overall theme of being visibly present and engaged no matter where he is or who he's working with.

### Employee Response

**Situation and Challenge:** While expanding GHAS trial eligibility (issue #1631) I first spiked a direct change to the Billing system (exploratory PR) assuming minimal extension would be fastest. A delayed Billing review surfaced deeper ownership, extensibility, and analytics concerns, invalidating the initial approach and forcing a pivot. Trial logic (counts, cooldowns, seat limits, dual systems) was more intricate than it appeared.

**Actions and Openness:**

- Turned implicit assumptions into explicit artifacts (issue hypotheses, draft ADR, final consolidation ADR)
- Embraced Billing feedback instead of defending sunk cost; shifted to SKUTrial (Growth-owned) for long-term agility
- Added explicit trial_end_date and trial_count design elements to fix analytics and cooldown integrity
- Phased migration (dual-read + feature flag + rollback triggers) to de-risk change
- Used Copilot for speed but adjusted pace so the team could digest architecture rather than just reviewing a large PR

**Growth Mindset and Learning:**

- Learned to front-load architecture (ADR first, spike second) in cross-team domains to avoid late reversals
- Velocity without shared understanding creates rework; I now pair AI acceleration with deliberate review checkpoints
- Clear ownership (Growth vs Billing) is a strategic choice, not just a technical one; unifying trials reduces future coordination tax
- Explicit lifecycle data (end dates) prevents hidden analytics debt

**Shaping Future Approach:** Will start complex initiatives with a concise problem/option matrix, schedule early stakeholder alignment, and use feature flags + dual reads as default for core migrations. AI output will be framed clearly as "exploration" until architecture is settled.

**Key Artifacts:** Problem framing: Issue #1631 / Spike: Early PR / Draft ADR: Eligibility ADR draft / Final architecture: Consolidation ADR

---

## Question 3: What are your goals for the upcoming period?

### Manager Response

Chanakya's goals are directionally right. First of all, he's right that there's some uncertainty about exactly what comes next beyond the trial work. That's on me and Dorothy to clarify as we get into the next planning cycle.

Completing the unified GHAS trial migration to SKUTrial and retiring the legacy path is the obvious next step given the work he did this cycle.

Working on Copilot for the Secret Risk Assessment is a great fit, and I think Chanakya is a natural choice for DRI when we work on that.

Extending self-serve GHAS trials to the Teams plan is a reasonable follow-on but falls into the category of stuff that is largely outside of his direct control. Allowing Team orgs to trial GHAS is a product decision that so far we've deprioritized given the amount of revenue potential there vs. improving the experience for enterprises. If this is something that can be accomplished as a side bet or hackathon project, let's make it happen, but I think it's unlikely to make it into our OKRs in the next few months unless things change.

### Employee Response

My upcoming goals build on the trial, security, and AI foundations I established. First, I will complete the unified GHAS trial migration to the SKUTrial model, retiring the legacy path. Next, I would like to work on extending self-serve GHAS trials to the Teams plan and refine the enablement experience as needed. I think with my experience on working on other teams and Copilot, working on the conversational assistant in the Secret Risk Assessment post-run banner seems like a good opportunity. It would be great to continue working on GHAS related projects but I'm not sure exactly where our priorities are and what we can realistically pick and execute on.

# FY26 H1 Review: Jennifer Ramirez

_Manager Reflection, FY26 Reflection_
_Period: June - December 2025 (FY26 H1)_

---

## Question 1: What results did you deliver, and how did you do it?

### Manager Response

Jennifer came back from leave in mid-June and hit the ground running. She was shipping PRs right from the start, which is rare. It (understandably) takes most people a while to get back into the groove and wrap their brains around what happened while they were out. Jennifer was productive from day 1, and her productivity has remained solid and steady throughout the rest of this review cycle. One peer put it simply: "Jennifer gets a lot of work done."

Jennifer is also extremely collaborative. I often observe Jennifer being the first person to offer to pair on a problem someone is struggling with. In this way, she's not only a force multiplier for the team, but she also is helping to create a collaborative and welcoming and supportive team culture via leading by example. Multiple teammates called it out as valuable and appreciated as well.

**Peer feedback:**

> "What I appreciate most is her openness and collaborative approach. She's been willing to jump on calls to talk through hairy technical decisions."

> "She has consistently asked for input on priorities while proactively identifying ways to improve both our trial and transition processes. Working with her has been great."

> "Every time I've reached out with a question, she's either had an answer or pointed me in the right direction."

> "When I first joined, she was the first person to share tips and tricks with me and jump into pairs, which made ramping up a lot easier."

> "She's always quick to help out, and I really appreciate how approachable and knowledgeable she is when someone needs support."

In terms of specific accomplishments, this half for Jennifer has been marked by consistent ownership over the trial and checkout experience, and the related billing implications. She has become our de facto owner of trials and checkout end to end, and is single-handedly responsible for many of the improvements we've made to trials since inheriting that experience into our AOR.

She improved the sales-serve trial system by working with the Revenue team to identify looser requirements for approved sales-serve trials, and then building them. She worked closely with Dan to redesign the API and allow for customers with minimal existing GHAS usage to trial. As a related note, this architecture is now being used as the foundation for self-serve trials as well, so that we can finally have all three trial types powered by the same backend, and Jennifer has been supporting this effort as well.

**Related peer feedback:**

> "She's worked closely with the external stakeholders (like leftrightleft) to get stuff like the Trials API smoothly shipped. Throughout the feature, she'd double-check any discovered assumptions with him."

She's had a huge impact on surprise billing escalations as well. The majority of the things that we've done to reduce surprise billing or accidental enablement have been done by Jennifer. This includes itemized checkout pages where customers can see and remove individual SKUs, building a trial reset flow that takes a snapshot of the customer's pre-trial state and reverts back to that when the trial expires, putting an end to automatic GHAS enablement and default security configurations, and more. As a result of Jennifer's work, support escalations related to GHAS billing have drastically reduced.

**Related peer feedback:**

> "I have really appreciated Jennifer's proactive approach toward identifying and remediating issues in our back-end systems. These problems are frequently very difficult for us in Revenue to identify before they become customer-facing issues."

### Employee Response

**Standardized and Expanded Trials Ecosystem:**

- **Early Delivery of Planned Improvements:** Completed the Q1 epic to set GitHub-recommended security configuration as the default for DFD GHAS trials ahead of schedule, accelerating value realization for our customers and exemplifying a Customer-Obsessed mindset.

- **Expanded Eligibility:** Over 75% of trial requests were previously rejected due to strict requirements. I partnered cross-functionally with the sales team to identify pain points and redesigned the trial creation API to support more nuanced eligibility criteria. This reduced friction and enabled more potential customers to experience GHAS, reflecting our Better Together and Customer-Obsessed values.

- **Removed Surprise Billing Risks:**
  - Eliminated silent enablement of paid security configurations in DFD trials by implementing explicit opt-in gating. As a result, there were 0 unintended private repo activations post-launch, reinforcing trust and strengthening compliance posture.
  - Simplified the checkout flow from more than 4 steps to just 2 and added a transparent pricing preview with explicit opt-out, further supporting our commitment to clarity and customer trust.
  - Utilized Copilot to research and document trial logic, which enabled the unification and extension of reset logic across all GHAS trial types. Now, every non-purchasing trial consistently reverts with the removal of paid GHAS SKUs, decreasing support burden and improving the experience for non-purchasing customers that trialed GHAS.

- **Improved Completion of Standalone GHAS Trial:** Introduced standardized trial completion logic so that all customers end in a clear and desirable state, making it easier to seamlessly purchase GHAS at the end of their trial or in the future.

---

## Question 2: Reflect on recent challenges

### Manager Response

Jennifer's documentation and written communication can be a bit too descriptive (explaining what was done) rather than reflective (explaining why specific approaches were chosen). To grow into more senior roles, she should focus on articulating her reasoning, the trade-offs considered, and the strategic rationale behind her technical decisions. This would help teammates learn from her choices and enable better collaboration on complex problems. The ADR for self-serve trials is a good example of something that could have benefitted from this, and likely would have gotten more/better feedback as a result.

Semi-related: the GHAS enablement PR she mentioned is minor but a good callout. Skipping the ADR process, even for a small change like this, meant missing an opportunity for broader team scrutiny of trust and billing implications, as she said. Our standard processes exist to catch these issues before they ship. That said, Jennifer owned the remediation fully and implemented comprehensive fixes, which showed her strong accountability and growth mindset. And Jennifer is one of the champions of process in the team, so I don't expect this to be a general problem. :D

Jennifer excels at executing what's asked of her, but as she progresses toward staff-level roles, she needs to shift from "doing great work on assigned tasks" to "identifying high-impact opportunities independently." This means looking for ways to increase her influence outside the immediate team, whether through cross-team initiatives, mentorship, knowledge sharing, or identifying strategic problems that aren't yet on anyone's roadmap.

Jennifer's push this quarter to formalize and unify the end-of-trial reset process is a great example. That's valuable work that we shipped only because Jennifer had the idea, made the plan, and pushed it through. I'd love to see more of this, and the trials domain knowledge she's built up is a great platform for it. Creating and sharing docs and running knowledge-sharing sessions would multiply her impact across the org.

### Employee Response

I streamlined work originally scoped as a Q1 epic into a single PR because it appeared simple. After release, we saw increased incidents of surprise billing: security configuration defaults silently enabled paid SKUs (e.g., GHAS features) and did not reset them when the trial ended. The intention was to increase SKU exposure and conversion during the Digital Front Door (DFD) trial. However, many participants were not actively trialing those capabilities and were surprised to be billed afterward.

Skipping an ADR reduced the opportunity for broader scrutiny and early detection of this trust and billing risk. I applied a growth mindset by fully owning remediation. I:

- Limited automatic enablement of GHR on public repositories (where usage is free).
- Added the ability to disable paid SKUs directly from the checkout page.
- Implemented a robust reset mechanism so paid SKUs revert unless explicitly purchased at trial end.

Going forward, I will treat even "small" changes with potentially large financial or trust impacts as design-worthy, using ADRs or lightweight design artifacts to enable early validation rather than relying on reactive correction.

---

## Question 3: What are your goals for the upcoming period?

### Manager Response

Goal 1 is exactly the type of thing I was talking about in the last paragraph of question 2! I'd LOVE to see that and share it far and wide. I support goals 2 and 3 as well. Shipping CSRA and consistent persistence would be two fantastic and impactful achievements for the 2nd half of the review cycle. Some of the objectives and success metrics I think are up for debate but there's no need to squabble about the details here. I'm fully aligned with the high level goals and the reasoning behind them.

### Employee Response

**Goal 1: Communicate Unified GHAS Trial Progress**

Create and circulate a concise "How Far We've Come" overview explaining the unification of GHAS trial flows, the shared architecture, and the standardized reset path, providing adjacent teams with a clear, consistent narrative.

**Objectives:**

- Consolidate the before/after view of trial architecture, highlighting the transition from fragmented to unified flow, which creates clarity around our processes.
- Summarize the core lifecycle of GHAS trials, including enablement, trial usage, reset, and post-trial state, aligning with our values of clarity and transparency.
- Develop a short FAQ for trial flows and paid SKU usage during and after trials.

**Evidence of Success:**

- FAQ is referenced by sales or support to help customers
- Less confusion on how trials work

**Goal 2: Lead the Code Security Risk Assessment Epic Toward Predictable, High-Quality Delivery**

Establish milestone-based testing and broaden visibility of the epic's progress so issues shift left and the tail-end cleanup phase is shorter and less isolated.

**Objectives:**

- Define milestone checkpoints each with explicit test scope.
- Assign ownership for automated and exploratory testing at each milestone.
- Maintain a living epic log or discussion thread for visibility.
- Share brief demos or written updates at a steady cadence with adjacent teams.
- Capture a lightweight defect profile (severity categories + when discovered) to compare early vs late discovery patterns.
- Reflect learnings post-release and codify into a reusable "milestone test gate" template.

**Evidence of Success:**

- Fewer defects discovered at the tail end of the project.
- Shorter and less isolated clean-up phase; engineers do not spend weeks cleaning up bugs after others have moved on.
- Milestone test template requested or reused in a subsequent epic.

**Goal 3: Advance Consistent Persistence of Security Product Enablements to Support Trial Conversions**

Implement consistent persistence model for security product enablements so Security Overview reliably reflects active states, reducing misconfiguration friction and creating a clearer, more trustworthy evaluation environment during trials.

**Objectives:**

- Partner with Steve to refine the ADR for consistent enablement state recording if needed.
- Ensure Security Configurations and Security Overview derives from a single reliable source of truth.
- Capture qualitative feedback and identify trial friction reductions for future iteration.

**Evidence of Success:**

- Fewer internal or support escalations citing mismatches between actual enablement and displayed state.
- Security Overview enablement state is not one of the primary pain points in trials anymore.
- Trial participants express less confusion about which security features are active.

# FY26 H1 Review: Nic

_Manager Reflection, FY26 Reflection_
_Period: June - December 2025 (FY26 H1)_

---

## Question 1: What results did you deliver, and how did you do it?

### Manager Response

Nic joined us in mid-June (about 6 weeks into this reflection cycle) and hit the ground running. He's consistently shown an impressive amount of impact, flexibility, productivity/efficiency, and proactive thinking, and he's demonstrating readiness to take on the SE2 role.

One of Nic's main areas of impact was the Pricing Calculator for the Secret Risk Assessment. He took the lead on the frontend implementation (which is especially notable as a still-new SE1) and handled the vast majority of the frontend work needed to ship this deceivingly large feature to both groups of metered customers. The feedback from the DRI (Steve) about Nic's work on the project was very positive.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Nic consistently picks up meaningful work and moves it forward decisively. He owned most of the front-end implementation for the secret risk assessment calculator."

He also pushed forward the CTA additions to the GHAS licensing page, navigating what was a poorly defined problem space to get something ready for review quickly.

Beyond his core deliverables, Nic has shown a proactive approach to code quality. He proposed and shipped cleanup improvements to the SRA calculators during our tech debt iteration, and he's been thoughtful about following React best practices even when the existing codebase doesn't consistently follow those patterns. He also made drive-by contributions to other teams' codebases, including adding Storybook stories to a UI library we don't own. When he noticed the local dev setup for SRA wasn't well documented, he wrote it up and helped teammates get unblocked.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Nic has done a great job quickly getting up to speed and contributing to the team's work. He has applied his knowledge of React to help us build and maintain a better UI. Nic should continue to provide his thoughts and insight on how we build using React."

What's been particularly impressive is Nic's willingness to work well outside his comfort zone. Despite having no prior Ruby or Rails experience, he single-handedly owned the end-of-trial reset workstream, which was heavily backend-focused. He added a new database table, ramped up on DB migrations, and even submitted a PR against the puppet repo for pt-archiver. He also picked up Golang work to get outside the JavaScript bubble, pairing with teammates to accelerate his learning.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Nic spends a lot of time outside of his comfort zone. And that's exactly how you expand your comfort zone. A clear (and recent) example is Nic diving into the Dotcom backend to identify the locations in code where surprise billing data might be introduced in the form of stale configuration entries."

> "Stepping into discomfort with vulnerability and curiosity is just the absolute best way to learn and grow. Nic does this consistently."

> "Navigating the Dotcom codebase to understand when/where *anything* happens is hard enough, and then when Ruby isn't your 'first' language it can be considerably more difficult. Then balancing simplicity and convention to hook into those events/locations is no easy feat either. Nic did these things with care, calling out the bits of his own solution that he wasn't happy with. Then through synchronous code review, we came to a more robust solution together."

> "Nic has begun to branch out into fixing bugs in our data through his work in stale trial data. This has furthered his knowledge into our product area and he should continue occasionally picking up work like this because it will only help him grow confidence needed to tackle work in any area we own."

Nic has been productive with minimal oversight, which is notable for an SE1 and demonstrates signs of SE2 readiness. He's communicative when priorities are unclear and asks for guidance when needed. He's also been leveraging AI tools practically, such as using Copilot for routine tasks and leaning on in-editor chat to understand unfamiliar code.

**Peer feedback:**

> "Nic's rapid ramp, consistent curiosity, and polite persistence ('How can I help?' backed by action) are real positives. He's already functioning above typical expectations for someone only five months into role tenure."

> "Nic used lightweight automations to bridge time zone gaps, by scheduling messages that flagged PRs ready for review before his day started. His habit of asking 'How can I be helpful?' translates into real momentum; he doesn't just volunteer, he follows through."

> "Nic brings great energy to the team and I'm happy he's here!"

### Employee Response

This period, I was lucky to work on some fun initiatives. I took ownership of the SRA pricing calculator's frontend, which currently has an 11% SRA conversation rate compared to 5% without usage of the pricing calculator. I also DRI'd the initiative to add CTAs to the GHAS licensing page, which gave existing GHAS users more ways to ingress to their configuration settings, and non-GHAS users more ways to start their journey with the product.

Beyond these epics, I actively sought out ways to contribute outside of just knocking out my assigned tasks and issues. When I saw fit, I made drive-by refactors and fixes in other codebases, such as by adding new Storybook stories to a different team's UI library, or by refactoring some prop drilling practices to follow React best practices. I also learned and documented the setup for running the SRA page in our local dev environment, which wasn't well described beforehand and ended up being useful for other teammates who worked on the pricing calculator.

Throughout this period, I found some ways to use AI to help out whenever needed. I used Copilot agent to handle small, more trivial and repetitive tasks and relied on the in-editor chat to help me understand unfamiliar code and business logic.

---

## Question 2: Reflect on recent challenges

### Manager Response

Nic identified his ramp-up on Ruby on Rails as a key challenge this period. Coming in with TypeScript and Java experience, the MVC architecture and Rails conventions were out of his comfort zone. I love how he's approached this, by treating code reviews as learning opportunities, asking questions on teammates' PRs, and using Copilot to accelerate his understanding of syntax and fundamentals. It obviously worked. He went from zero Rails experience to owning a backend-heavy workstream quickly. He treated it as a growth opportunity and attacked it head on rather than pushing to focus on more familiar languages. His Golang work and diving into FR duties further demonstrates this growth mindset.

Outside of that, one theme from peer feedback is the importance of taking another pass at his PRs before opening them up for review. This could mean making sure he fully understands and is able to explain every line of code, which is easy to neglect in the age of AI. It could also mean making a working solution even better (less complex, faster, more maintainable). The initial working solution often isn't the best one, and iterating on a solution to improve it and more deeply understand the code itself and the tradeoffs involved improve the work and also show respect for the time of the people reviewing the code.

**Peer feedback:**

> "After Nic puts a solution in place, he should take the time to think one level deeper. If enqueueing a new job from three different locations in the monolith, consider simplifying to an instrumentation-driven pub/sub strategy where we only enqueue the job in one location and we keep code ownership boundaries clearer. If introducing a new query, consider the P99 use cases; will this query be too large or slow for a huge enterprise or organization? What steps can you take to give yourself and others confidence in your solution?"

> "One of Nic's tests stubbed a method which didn't exist, creating warnings when running the tests. In this situation, it should have been clear (from the warning) that something wasn't right. Nic, with help from others, could and should have figured out what was wrong and addressed it before this pull request was submitted for review and ultimately deployed. This problem is nearly impossible to detect in code review, so Nic was in the unique position of raising the concern prior to approval and deployment."

> "Nic's solutions are solid. I'd like to see him spend more time in the refactor (and reflect) step, searching for ways to improve an already solid solution into a great solution."

> "If something doesn't look or feel right, take the time to ask questions, gain understanding, and address the concern until it does look and feel right, or you're at least clear on the trade-offs."

Another growth area is around shipping work completely and tracking his work past the point of merging the PR. I've seen a few cases where work lingered unshipped or sat behind a feature flag for a couple weeks until someone asked about it. Nothing is truly "done" until it's in front of 100% of users, and building habits around tracking feature flag cleanup would help here.

### Employee Response

One of the biggest challenges I ran into was that the GitHub monolith's backend uses Ruby on Rails. Before GitHub, my backend experience was primarily in TypeScript and Java in an AWS IaC infrastructure. The MVC architecture and Rails syntax both felt completely foreign, and for a bit it felt like I was learning to code all over again.

To try and overcome this, I spent a great deal of time using Copilot in my IDE to understand Rails fundamentals and syntax, while also treating code reviews as a learning opportunity. I would frequently ask questions on my teammates' PRs to deepen my understanding. The experience made me a bit more comfortable with being a novice again and reinforced how learning new paradigms makes me a more well-rounded engineer. This Rails experience is still a work in progress and I still feel like I have lots to learn.

I applied the same growth mindset when I volunteered to pick up work in Golang. I had never worked with such a low-level language in a production environment, but I again saw it as a chance to push myself and grow my skillset. This time, in addition to relying on Copilot to explain syntax and fundamentals, I paired with Steve who was able to pick apart my code and show me areas for improvement.

Finally, I dipped into FR duties, another area that was completely new to me. I deliberately sought this out to sharpen my investigative and debugging skills, as I didn't get much investigative work at my previous jobs. Pairing with Alex and Chanakya was very valuable for learning the ropes and establishing a baseline for what to expect.

---

## Question 3: What are your goals for the upcoming period?

### Manager Response

Nic has set three solid goals for the upcoming period, and they align well with what I see as his next steps towards SE2. Here's my attempt to refine them and add some accountability:

**1. Lead a medium-sized, customer-facing project end-to-end**

This is exactly the right focus. The key difference between SE1 and SE2 is that SE1s implement solutions others design, while SE2s help design solutions and take ownership of small-to-medium features end-to-end. Nic has proven he can execute reliably on assigned work; now he needs opportunities to practice planning, decomposition, and coordination beyond a solo scope.

Refined: In Q3, DRI an epic that involves at least 2 engineers (including Nic) or requires coordination with another team. Success means shipping to 100% of users on schedule, with Nic owning the project plan, coordinating the work, and driving decisions without needing anyone to step in.

Next step: We'll identify a suitable epic together by the end of January, and we'll check in on progress and feedback during our 1:1s.

**2. Become an independent FR and contribute to system reliability**

Nic has already started shadowing FR shifts and pairing with teammates to learn the investigative process. Transitioning to confidently managing rotations independently is the obvious next step.

Refined: Complete at least 3 rotations independently by end of cycle. "Independent" means resolving or escalating issues appropriately without needing a shadow or significant hand-holding.

Next step: Schedule Nic a regular FR shift in early Q3 and assign a secondary FR who can be on hand for support.

**3. Proactively improve code quality and system integrity by identifying and fixing sources of bugs**

I like the intent here, though I'd get more specific on the scope. Diving into ambiguous problem spaces can slow velocity at a career stage where consistent delivery matters. This goal should focus on building product knowledge through targeted bug fixes rather than open-ended investigation.

Refined: Identify and fix at least 3 root-cause issues related to FR issues/support escalations by end of cycle. Each fix should include a brief write-up of what caused the issue and how to prevent similar problems in the future.

Next step: Check in at 1:1s during the first FR shift mentioned in the previous goal to identify the right issue(s) to dive into in order to achieve this goal.

### Employee Response

1. **Lead a medium sized, customer-facing project end-to-end to drive business impact:** I want to take on the role of DRI for a new feature or enhancement. Success can be measured by its successful delivery to 100% of users, and key metrics can include positive customer feedback or positive business metrics.

2. **Become an independent FR and contribute to the ongoing improvement of system reliability:** I want to transition from shadowing FR shifts to confidently managing them myself. This includes independently investigating issues, identifying root causes and applying solutions. Success can be measured by joining the FR rotation and successfully completing a few rotations.

3. **Proactively improve code quality and system integrity by identifying and fixing sources of bugs:** I want to gain a better understanding of the products and code areas that we own, so that I can work towards identifying and fixing sources of bugs, particularly those related to billing discrepancies or other incidents we often receive. Success can be measured by a reduction in incidents and customer complaints.

# FY25 H1 Performance Review — Steve Richert

_Review period: May 4, 2024 to November 17, 2024_
_Source: Reflections Questions export (PDF + XLSX)_

## Question 1: Key accomplishments

### Manager response

**Enablement platform planning**

Steve was leading the Security Products enablement platform initiative before being moved into Copilot PLG. The vision and architecture work demonstrated good strategic thinking. He took on the challenge of unifying previously scattered approaches to security features, starting out with a focus on consistent persistence. This manifested in a giant ADR and a somewhat less giant EDR, which Steve then shared around widely to get buy-in across all teams owning security products.

Related peer feedback:
- "Steve has done a fantastic job articulating the vision, getting consensus, and laying the groundwork for this huge initiative."

## Question 1: Key accomplishments

### Manager response

Steve has been a driving force behind Copilot chat shareability from inception to our finishing touches. His impact went far beyond code contributions, as he took on a true DRI role (not just a GitHubby "the person who writes the issues" DRI role) by writing the product spec and consistently pushing this feature forward through numerous challenges.

Notable technical artifacts:

- Shared chat ID format ADR (especially crunchy, demonstrates technical complexity)
