Steve ramped up on Go and owned the vast majority of our CAPI work. He created many new endpoints for shared chats and then pushed forward and singlehandedly implementing Alive-based live streaming despite stakeholder pushback that it would be too difficult or not worth the effort.

What's particularly impressive is how Steve navigated the product ambiguity in the Copilot space. He didn't just write code - he helped shape product direction from the ground up. Our Copilot PM (Kelly) relied on him heavily for nearly all product planning, showing his ability to span both engineering and product domains effectively.

Relevant peer feedback:
- "Steve ramped up on Go very quickly and made significant contributions to CAPI. He also offered to walk the team through any changes to help us review the PRs and also learn API development in Go. I appreciated the in-depth understanding of CAPI in such short time and the work on endpoints allowed me to move quickly on building the UI."
- "Steve did an amazing job writing ADRs (Share conversations and thread shared id format) The in-depth analysis on shared thread is very fascinating and I'm grateful that he decided to put that into writing!"

I should note that Steve was also asked to pivot away from his security product enablement platform work before that effort could realize its impact. This was due to organizational priorities shifting, not any issue with Steve's execution.

### Employee response

Since the last reflection, I've been deep in the Copilot chat space, building out thread sharing, thinking through edge cases, and generally doing what I could to solidify some pretty vague product direction. A lot of that work lived in the CAPI layer (where I've become the team SME), but a good chunk was upstream product shaping, architecture alignment, and poking product holes. Some highlights:

- Wrote on and implemented shared thread ID formats
- Spiked and estimated several ideas for early product shaping and design
- Kept up a steady stream of meaningful PRs, docs, and internal nudges that helped shape the work beyond just the code

I always try to stay intentional about security throughout, as evidenced by my involvement in the security review of our now-adopted shared thread ID format.

## Question 2: Growth mindset / areas of opportunity

### Manager response

Steve exemplifies growth mindset in how he approaches new technical challenges. As he mentioned in his self-reflection, he was "ripped from the cozy comfort of Ruby, Rails, and MySQL" and thrust into Go, Cosmos DB, and React - none of which he previously knew. Now he's become the team's go-to resource for CAPI (Go and Cosmos DB) while also making significant strides with React.

What's particularly valuable is Steve's ability to navigate product ambiguity. As he said, he's gotten better at quickly spiking ideas, evaluating their merit, and moving on from those that don't work without getting attached. This ability to hold plans loosely while still driving forward is exactly what we need in the PLG space where experimentation is king (or, at least, should be).

Relevant peer feedback:
- "Steve has been resilient and incredibly adaptive. In the space of the past few months, Steve has: Ramped up on Golang and contributed to major functionality pieces to sharing threads backend. Without his contributions on that front we would not have shipped on time. Been a voice of reason when demands/constraints change on the fly by interfacing with product and design adeptly."
- "Steve juggled multiple personalities this cycle where he was wrangling with messy product requirements, pushing new code changes, keeping up with the team changes, learning React as we go, etc. I think he did great sorting out the unseen / not the most visible (but important) work which helped the team focus on building quickly."

Looking forward, I'm excited to see how Steve applies these skills to our more familiar security product initiatives, which will benefit from his ability to clarify engineering focus amidst product ambiguity (which will still be there, no doubt).

### Employee response

This reflection cycle was full of experiments, some of which paid off, and others that didn't get shipped but still helped us learn. I've gotten better at spiking and tossing ideas that don't land, and I've gotten more comfortable helping shape product direction from the ground up, sometimes starting from an itty bitty newborn idea. There was a ton of product ambiguity this cycle and I learned loads around how to live in that ambiguity and hold plans more loosely.

I also just learned a ton technically. I applied my growth mindset to this new product space and I don't think I would have had nearly as much success if I couldn't. I was ripped from the cozy comfort of Ruby, Rails, and MySQL. Then I was thrust into the world of Go, Cosmos DB, and React, none of which I knew. Now I'm the go-to for CAPI (Go, and Cosmos) and I've made great strides in learning and better intuiting React.

I think that PLG comes with a larger portion of product ambiguity than I've been used to with more traditional feature development. And the Copilot swarm variety of PLG was especially chaotic. But the ability to navigate that chaos will help me clarify our engineering focus and purpose as we dive into PLG initiatives in security products.

## Question 3: Development opportunities

### Manager response

Steve has identified some excellent development opportunities. Continuing to deepen his React knowledge will be valuable as we work on security products, and his approach of taking a recommended course is solid. Similarly, revisiting that Go book now that he has practical experience is a great idea.

While Steve has delivered exceptional results within the team context, one area for continued growth is expanding his sphere of influence beyond our immediate team boundaries. As a staff engineer, Steve's expected to drive meaningful impact across multiple teams or even org-wide initiatives.

## Question 1: What results did you deliver, and how did you do it?

### Manager Response

The feature shipped to all metered customers and we're now building on top of that work for the Quick Setup experience and the Code Security Risk Assessment. His thorough planning and technical leadership (including performance investigations and a great ADR which I've since used as an example for other folks) stood out. He also used this as an opportunity to support Nic's growth, by giving Nic problems to solve outside of his wheelhouse and pairing with him/responding to his questions to keep him unblocked and moving.

Beyond the pricing calculator, Steve also shipped a system to prevent surprise billing caused by stale configs. This work automatically finds and clears bad data before it can charge customers, which has almost definitely prevented some surprise billing scenarios already, judging by how those escalations rapidly dropped off when it shipped. This isn't the most exciting or flashy work, but these incidents stole a HUGE amount of time and energy from us and cost us a lot of customer goodwill over the past couple quarters. If Steve's work prevents even one new surprise billing incident then that is dozens or hundreds of hours of company time (and customer frustration) that he saved.

His approach to this problem was incredibly thoughtful and thorough. He mapped out every possible combination of billing model and configuration entries so that we could all align on which are valid and which are problematic. He then built a monitoring job and Datadog dashboard to keep an eye on them, shipped a transition to clean them up, and iterated on that monitoring job to make it a cleanup job. With any luck, Steve made the "overbilling as a result of stale config" problem obsolete.

He's also currently unblocking Apple's GHAS rollout by tracking down a concurrency issue he had predicted months earlier, building a locking mechanism to fix it, and backporting that solution across multiple GHES versions. This directly supports and unblocks the GHAS rollout of one of our largest customers.

Going out of order, but it's also worth mentioning that early in the cycle, Steve wrapped up important shared conversation work for Copilot PLG and then shifted to staff augmentation on the ESM project. He delivered multiple UI improvements and refactors in an unfamiliar space with a new team, and rolled off when it became clear that the work didn't require his seniority and other projects could benefit more from it.

I also want to call out Steve's role as a cultural leader on the team. Big Orca is "Big Orca" because he pushed for it relentlessly. He created our swag store and designed everything in there. This kind of team pride makes us feel like we're a part of something, like we're an actual team instead of a group of people working on the same stuff. ### Employee Response

**Quantify your impact**

I delivered meaningful impact across two large product areas this period: Copilot PLG and Enterprise Security.

On the Copilot side, I wrapped up the shared conversation work so that the experience was stable, predictable, and ready for long-term growth. That included tightening the frontend behavior around shared threads, hardening edge cases, and removing the last feature flags standing between Copilot PLG and a clean, reliable sharing workflow.

After that, I shifted to enterprise security and quickly became a key contributor on the new Enterprise Security Manager (ESM) effort. Even though that team needed a lot of frontend monolith engineering rather than backend security work, I leaned in and delivered multiple UI improvements, refactors, and consistency upgrades across the emerging ESM experience.

I also shipped high-impact platform work outside any product mandate. I expanded the HTML pipeline's image sizing support, added missing capabilities to the Markdown alert system, optimized a couple of tight backend spots (batching Repository#archived?, fixing script/bootstrap --pristine), and improved asset-restriction behavior. These were quality-of-life improvements that removed friction for maintainers and customers without being on any roadmap.

The most consequential ship this period was leading the engineering work for the Secret Protection pricing calculator and bulk enablement flow. I scoped the work, wrote 77 issues, aligned six engineers, partnered closely with design, and delivered the whole project across monolith changes, query-layer improvements, and a new Twirp endpoint. That feature now gives enterprises clarity on cost and a safe path to adopt GHAS features at scale.

I also solved a long-standing customer pain point by building the system that prevents "surprise billing" caused by stale state. That work now automatically finds and clears bad data before it can charge customers, significantly reducing escalation load and protecting revenue integrity.

Finally, I'm unblocking Apple's GHAS rollout by tracking down a concurrency issue I had predicted months earlier, building a locking mechanism to fix it, and backporting that solution across multiple GHES versions. This directly supports one of our largest customers and helps ensure GHAS can succeed at massive scale.

**Leverage of AI**

I used Copilot consistently to augment my work across languages and stacks. When I was ramping up in Go for Copilot PLG, Copilot helped me bootstrap idiomatic implementations so I could focus on design rather than syntax. Throughout the reflection period, I used Copilot's automated PR review to tighten code quality and catch oversights early.

When planning new work, I created Copilot Spaces seeded with design docs, code references, and repository context. This gave me a single AI-aware surface where I could explore approaches, validate architecture decisions, and keep the full problem space in view. I also used Copilot as a guide when navigating unfamiliar parts of the monolith, prompting it for conventions, patterns, and best practices, which helped me deliver frontend and Rails improvements more efficiently than if I had relied solely on manual exploration.

**Security contributions**

Most of my work this period directly supported GitHub's security posture. The Secret Protection pricing calculator and bulk enablement features create safer paths for customers to adopt advanced security capabilities, reducing configuration errors and improving visibility. The stale billing cleanup system protects customers from unintended financial exposure and helps GitHub maintain trust, especially for large enterprise accounts with complex billing histories.

My concurrency and locking work for the Apple GHAS rollout was also critical security infrastructure. Without reliable enablement at scale, large enterprises cannot adopt GHAS safely. I identified the flaw, delivered the fix, and have been steadily backporting it so our largest customers can move forward without risk.

The ESM project itself is a security initiative, and my contribution there strengthened the UI and UX around enterprise-level role and permission management. Across all these projects, I approached the work through a security-first lens: correctness, predictability, and auditability.

**Culture in action**

I leaned into Customer-Obsessed thinking when building the surprise-billing prevention system, focusing on a root cause that customers couldn't see but deeply felt. The work wasn't glamorous, but it eliminated a real source of pain and protected trust.

Ship to Learn showed up in how I handled the pricing calculator project. Rather than try to design the perfect system upfront, I created small, reviewable pieces, shipped continuously, and adjusted direction as new constraints surfaced.

I embodied a Growth Mindset by jumping into Go development, deepening my frontend skills in the monolith, and tackling large cross-stack problems outside my comfort zone. I treated each new area as an opportunity to grow rather than a blocker.

Own the Outcome guided my approach to the Apple GHAS issue. I didn't just file concerns or propose a fix. I delivered it, validated it, and took responsibility for the downstream GHES backports.

Finally, Better Together defined my work on the pricing calculator and ESM. The best parts of both projects came from tight collaboration with design, product, and multiple engineering partners, ensuring the output was stronger than any single contributor.

## Question 2: Reflect on recent challenges

### Manager Response

On the Pricing Calculator, while the project shipped successfully, there were some things that could have gone smoother. Testing end to end and manually comparing to designs before the bug bash would have caught obvious issues (like the missing 3rd page) earlier. That way, the bug bash could have focused on breaking things in more obscure ways instead, and the ship date may have had less delay.

**Peer feedback:**

> "[Steve could] strengthen milestone quality gates to shift small findings earlier. On the secret risk assessment calculator, several minor issues appeared only at bug bash. A lightweight 'Milestone Done' checklist, upfront red tests for edge behaviors, and a brief milestone QA issue per milestone would surface these sooner and shrink the post-bash tail."

There was also a stretch early in the cycle where Steve wasn't shipping code related to the project he was leading. There should be time for a DRI to lead the work and also own some of it, and if there isn't, then that may be a flag that something else is wrong. For example, maybe the DRI is being forced to act as PM and define a more detailed product spec? Maybe the DRI is spending all of their time supporting the other people working on it, which could point to a possible performance issue with those people? Maybe the DRI is going too deep into defining a perfect plan for the complete solution instead of focusing on building a lean MVP and iterating on it with shippable milestones? Etc. Steve should speak up whenever he feels like he isn't able to write code for extended periods, so that I (his manager) can either help make that possible or validate that it's not an issue.

Outside of that, one thing we've discussed this half is the 2-3 week ramp-up period that happens when he jumps into a new problem space. Steve has acknowledged that he struggles with jumping into hard problems quickly, and this is a core staff engineer expectation. His strength in deep immersion over time is big and valuable, but it needs to be balanced with the ability to make faster progress in unfamiliar territory. Otherwise we'll keep running into these gaps in impact whenever he shifts to something new, which will continue to happen.

Communication (particularly with Slack) also came up this cycle. Steve shared that staying on top of Slack doesn't come naturally to him, and he recognizes this can limit his awareness of problems or opportunities happening across the organization. We talked about how setting aside dedicated time to catch up on channels could help surface situations where his expertise would be valuable. This aligns with peer feedback noting that it can sometimes be hard to reach Steve or get him to respond to something. More active engagement with Slack would improve both his accessibility to colleagues and his ability to identify areas where he can have broader impact.

**Peer feedback:**

> "While Steve's knowledge and skills are top notch, it's sometimes hard to get a hold of him! Occasionally it can be hard for me to get a response from him on Slack, even if his calendar appears free. This has sometimes led to delayed PR reviews or pairs. I can understand that he's working on things outside of our team's scope, so maybe more visibility into what he's working on could be helpful so we can have insights into whether he's swamped or not."

I also received some (verbal, so I can't provide it verbatim) peer feedback that Steve sometimes gets negative in team discussions, expressing frustrations or feelings of helplessness about the company in ways that can make folks anxious or stressed. Being a leader as a staff IC is complex, and I want Steve to spend more time thinking about how he wants to lead the team and which aspects of his experience are good fits for team discussions versus 1:1s with me. It's healthy for Steve to model psychological safety, but his words carry additional weight as the most senior engineer on the team. People often look to him for how to feel about things. If he can bring frustrations to our 1:1s and project a sense of ownership in team settings, even when facing crappy situations, that may help with overall team morale.

Finally, in terms of staff-level impact: we should continue to work on moving past "Steve gets assigned a solution" (such as Pricing Calculator) and moving toward getting assigned a problem or problem area (such as "stale config entries are causing surprise billing", and "Apple's having performance issues rolling out GHAS"), or even not getting assigned anything and identifying for himself where he can have the biggest impact. This is one of those "staff engineers should proactively identify their own areas for expanding impact" things, and it's one way of addressing the reality that we don't have a business need for a staff engineer on just our team alone. As we've discussed, I'd love for Steve to feel more empowered and encouraged to push for the things he thinks are important to work on, even (especially) if others aren't immediately receptive to it, or just do it himself without asking permission.

### Employee Response

One of the biggest challenges this period was shifting suddenly from a backend-heavy Copilot project to a frontend-heavy staff-augmentation role on ESM. It tested my adaptability and my ability to deliver high-quality work in a part of the stack that wasn't historically my strength.

I approached the unfamiliar UI patterns, design systems, and monolith frontend conventions with curiosity rather than frustration. I asked questions, sought feedback on early PRs, and treated each review as a chance to sharpen my understanding. I also wrote clear, thoughtful PR descriptions to ensure alignment and reduce rework.

Another challenge was tackling the stale billing issue, which had a lot of hidden complexity involving state transitions, billing rules, and production data that customers depended on. I had to balance safety, urgency, and correctness. I iterated carefully, peer-reviewed my own assumptions multiple times, and validated each step across environments. Solving that problem reinforced a habit of pausing, orienting, and validating assumptions before executing, an approach I will carry forward.

These experiences sharpened my resilience and strengthened my confidence in taking on ambiguous, cross-stack, and legacy-heavy projects.

---

## Question 3: What are your goals for the upcoming period?

### Manager Response

No notes! I'm aligned with all of these goals.

### Employee Response

1. **Successfully support the GHAS rollout for large enterprises, including Apple.** Deliver all required GHES backports, validate safety at scale, and reduce related escalation volume by at least 50 percent by the end of the period.

2. **Lead a cross-team security feature from design to ship.** Drive a security-focused project (pricing, enablement, or detection-related) from initial problem framing to production launch, with clear success metrics and customer impact.

3. **Improve the reliability and observability of the billing and enablement systems.** Reduce false-positive billing states and improve signal-to-noise in related alerts, aiming for at least a 30 percent reduction in manual debugging time for partner teams.

4. **Strengthen my technical influence within the organization.** Share at least two design docs or architectural proposals that help clarify confusing areas of the monolith or unify patterns across security features.

5. **Grow my leadership presence by mentoring and unblocking others.** Provide consistent support to at least two engineers on adjacent teams, helping them ramp up on security systems, billing logic, or monolith patterns.

## 1\. What results did you deliver, and how did you do it?

### Manager

Tara's work on unbundling the legacy GHAS trial was one of our most important epics this cycle. The work shipped quickly thanks to Tara's thorough prep and research, along with their scope cutting suggestions and focus on the end goal (i.e., killing the legacy trial) without getting distracted at all the other shiny things we could have done along the way. Their ADR struck a good balance between being short enough that it's not overwhelming to review but deep enough that all of the important architectural decisions got talked about. It clearly worked, because there were no major technical surprises or gotchas during execution.

**Relevant peer feedback:**

-   "Tara excels at their ability to take on complex initiatives and break them down into clear, focused components, including defining the problem statement, identifying current and technical issues, outlining target solutions, and suggesting related tasks that could be addressed in parallel. An example is the work to replace the legacy GHAS trial workflow. Tara clarified the core problems and mapped out a solution so the team knew exactly what was required. Tara also proactively suggested other improvements, like enabling Azure payment option, which was additional to the initial requirements and will have a meaningful impact."
-   "Tara owned the trial conversion end-to-end, clear scope, timely decisions, and openness to revisiting descoped items. Their balance of speed and willingness to course-correct made the project land smoothly and still protect future alignment, and they documented the items that were initially descoped so nothing was lost."
-   "Tara has been great at being one of the people somehow managing to keep all the context for unbundled GHAS skus in their head. Equally importantly though, they're taking that experience and really shaping upcoming features to be better! The work they did in deprecating legacy trials was really really well done."

On the ops front, Tara overhauled how the team tracks unhandled errors for the Apply job, then wrote handling for the most common existing errors and shipped playbooks on how to deal with new failure reasons in the future. This has played a big part in making our ops channel high signal low noise for perhaps the first time in our team's history. Tara's technical investigation skills were a lifesaver this cycle as well. They saved our bacon on multiple urgent and hairy GHES escalations, thoroughly documented steps to reproduce, and added the knowledge gained to our playbooks. They investigated latency issues with actions runner checks and made a Datadog notebook that the team has since referenced multiple times. Tara also stepped up to take ownership of the SAP outage given the urgency of the issue, despite not being the FR at the time. Finally, they started making more use of Copilot in this space, such as having agents remove feature flags and write an EMU test coverage script that identifies testing gaps which is useful company-wide.

Culturally, Tara has continued to operate as a technical leader who balances shipping with leveling up the team. They stepped up to handle FR duties for MULTIPLE MONTHS during a team transition and mentored Alex to build her FR skills. That mentoring was so beneficial to Alex's expertise and confidence that she has become one of our most productive first responders. Tara also systematized knowledge sharing via new sharing corner meetings and creating artifacts like Datadog walkthroughs and discussion posts to help the team. Peers consistently highlighted Tara's ability to engage honestly and constructively in team discussions as well, which is a good example for a team that struggles with ruinous empathy at times.

**Relevant peer feedback:**

-   "Tara consistently engages and gives actionable feedback during meetings. For example, their contribution to the roadmap sessions include thoughtful feedback on proposals and creative new ideas. The feedback is always framed constructively with ideas and offers to help. This makes the feedback helpful and also fosters a spirit of collaboration within the team."
-   "I appreciate Tara's approachable style and how they treat feedback as a tool, not a threat."

### Employee

Drove improvements in operational areas leading to greater reliability and more team documentation:

-   Handled FR for a while after team was impacted by layoffs
-   Improved how we tracked "unhandled" errors for the Apply job and burned down existing unhandled errors such that new errors are actionable. Wrote playbook on how to handle new unhandled errors.
-   Mentored Alex for a bit around FR, leading to her improving her ability to have successful FR shifts
-   Wrote a team playbook on how to handle a SEV1 page after being paged twice

**AI:**

-   Used Copilot agent to remove numerous feature flags
-   Had Copilot write a script to identify problematic EMU tests

**Broadening my impact:**

-   Wrote a discussion post to share how I fixed a GHES upgrade issue
-   Led 2 Big Orca Sharing corners

**Unbundled the GHAS trial:**

-   Wrote ADR on unbundling the GHAS trial
-   Wrote issues and kept project on track

**Culture in action:**

-   22 hours of volunteer work logged in Benevity

---

## 2\. Reflect on recent challenges: what did you learn and how did you apply a growth mindset?

### Manager

As discussed in previous 1-1s, Tara is an excellent debugger, and there is a risk that they remain the only person on the team capable of solving some of our most complex issues (especially GHES related issues) if we don't take proactive steps to avoid that. In future issues/escalations, I'd love to see Tara move beyond solving the problem to teaching others how to solve the problem. Their successful mentoring of Alex is a good example of the impact that can come from this.

**Relevant peer feedback:**

-   "Tara's deep GHES expertise is a clear asset; the most impactful focus area now is scaling that knowledge so the team is more self-sufficient."
-   "Today, when a GHES issue pops up, the default pattern is 'ask Tara,' which works, but concentrates context and creates interruption load."
-   "Setting up lightweight, repeatable sharing would raise everyone's baseline and reduce single-expert risk."

Regarding Tara's reflection on setting boundaries during FR shifts, I fully support the need to move away from a "get everything done" mentality. Continuing to prioritize sustainable emotional health and asking for prioritization help when overwhelmed is absolutely the right approach, and I'd love to help with this goal in any way that I can.

This focus is already getting noticed by peers, judging from this piece of feedback: "As someone on their team, I also deeply appreciate that Tara is extremely clear on boundaries. What they know, when they're able to meet, what they're able to do, having explicit asks...etc."

And also related to the pursuit of working sustainably, this peer feedback stands out:

"I personally think \[Tara should focus on\] finding a project that is a bit outside our team's comfort zone, that's trying to move too fast. I think these projects could really benefit from folks like Tara who can appropriately slow them down to think a bit more sustainably. Most of the projects that come to our team will be somewhat bread & butter. They can still have a huge impact (i.e. the legacy trial work), but I think Tara has shown they can work in more unknown spaces like the unbundling project."

### Employee

A challenge for me is setting boundaries around work during FR shifts. I typically respond to stress and overwhelm by just wanting to "get everything done". That works when I have a high degree of autonomy over the responsibilities I take on. However FR shifts directly challenge that response as I have much less autonomy over my work and it's just not feasible to just "get everything done".

I'm working on noticing when I'm feeling overwhelmed and challenging myself to take a break, go for a walk, and remember that nobody is asking to "get everything done" and that's not an expectation that is real. Additionally I can ask help for in prioritizing my work and identify what can wait for later.

---

## 3\. What are your goals for the upcoming period?

### Manager

Let's talk about if there are specific SMART goals to set here so that we can have plans and accountability.

### Employee

-   Increase visibility outside of team
-   Find things I can write about

checkins.content:
# Monthly check-in: 2026-03
_Chanakya Valluri, March 2026_

## Accomplishments

**CSRA UI work.** Shipped a solid batch of CSRA features: wired enable links to code security endpoints and per-repo dialog ([PR](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16447)), built out the repo table with clickable repo links and code security enabled state ([PR](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16887)), updated the security assessments page behind the feature flag ([PR](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15612)), and included repository_id in the CSRA assessment response ([PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423896)).

**Refactoring push.** Extracted concerns and sub-components from the EnablementDialog ([PR](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16440)), refactored the SecurityConfiguration model and added a Copilot instructions file for the backend ([PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422948)), and cleaned up CSRA models and RiskAssessmentsController for readability ([PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422949)). These refactors improved maintainability across the CSRA and SRA packages.

**Bug fixes and cleanup.** Fixed coverage stats not visible to org admins and security managers ([PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422052)), fixed usage cards not displaying on team orgs ([PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423185)), removed the `ghas_config_usage_cards` feature flag from both repos ([github](https://github.com/github/github/pull/424788), [github-ui](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/17064)).

**Discussion post on agentic workflows.** Wrote and published ["Rethinking how we execute feature work"](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/discussions/2694), proposing the team shift from horizontal FE/BE splits to vertical slices organized by user flows, with lighter DRI roles and issues focused on user scenarios rather than implementation steps. Articulated the shift from code-as-bottleneck to coordination-as-bottleneck clearly. Mike read it twice and discussed it with AI. The ideas were well-reasoned and largely non-controversial.

**Autopilot tool.** Continued building the tool on his own time. Hardened it against Copilot code review agent loops (caught a flip-flopping optional field bug that cycled four times), added issue link support, and is working toward the chaining feature where the agent processes a full epic with sub-issues and splits output into reviewable PRs.

**Figma MCP exploration.** Proactively explored connecting Figma designs via MCP on his own. Successfully pulled CSRA designs into chat. Identified the tooling gap (free tier limited to 6 tool calls vs. 200 on dev entitlements) and surfaced it as a team opportunity.

**Code reviews tripled.** 30 PRs reviewed this month, up from 19 last month and 10 in January. This was a specific growth area called out in prior check-ins and it meaningfully improved. Reviews spanned team repos (github, github-ui, airflow-sources, secrets-federation, datadog-monitoring) and included substantive comments, not just rubber stamps.

**Peer recognition from Steve.** Steve sent unsolicited feedback via Slack DM: "Chanakya is setting himself up as the local AI expert and that's exactly where you want to be. He's been generous with his sharing/teaching on the subject, plus consistent with bringing it back to the team and trying to incorporate the best bits into our process. We should all be emulating his trajectory right now." Steve also asked why Chanakya isn't senior yet.

**FR sync facilitation.** Taking over facilitating the next two FR syncs while Nic is on PTO. Also shadowed the SC3 interview loop prep meeting.

## Concerns

**Recurring scope shrinkage problem.** GHAS billing was supposed to be Chanakya's next big project, but the licensing team already built the UI and integration layer. The remaining work is too small to be meaningful. This is the latest in a pattern: Chanakya keeps getting assigned work that shrinks in scope before he can dig in. He said "I'm more than disappointed. I'm alarmed. I just feel like this keeps happening to me." Not a performance issue on his part, but it's eroding his motivation and needs active management attention.

**Satisfaction volatility.** Dipped earlier in the month (anxiety, feeling a lack of growth) but improved by March 19 thanks to the Autopilot side project. Having a "side quest" going is clearly important for his engagement. The Q4 roadmap is thin on technically interesting work, which is a risk.

**Coordination gap on CSRA.** Picked up an issue without full context on what Jennifer was working on, leading to a button being wired to the wrong dialog and a refactor that blocked a demo Dorothy had planned. Root cause: fast pace, no sync before picking up related work, ~12 issues with no clear visibility into who's doing what. Jennifer flagged this. Not a pattern yet, but worth watching.

**Wife's job loss.** Rithika was let go after 5 months at a new company, no severance. She had received a warning at 3 months, worked 10-hour days to improve, seemed like things were going well, then was let go unexpectedly. Adding personal stress.

**Quiet in team discussions.** Was mostly quiet during the FR sync technical discussions. Confirmed familiarity with the mismatched configs process when asked, but didn't contribute much beyond that. Camera still consistently off in meetings (flagged last month).

## Goal progress

| Goal | Status | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Ship hard billing budgets with thorough planning | 🔴 Blocked | GHAS billing scope shrank dramatically. Licensing team built the UI and integration layer. Remaining work reassigned to Nic when he returns from PTO. Chanakya needs a new meaty project. |
| New trial "get started" tasks | 🟡 Tentative | Tentatively assigned as Chanakya's Q4 project (onboarding tiles experience). Best opportunity on a thin roadmap. Not started yet. |
| Increase code review participation | 🟢 Improved | 30 reviews this month, up from 19 in Feb and 10 in Jan. Substantive reviews across multiple repos. Keep it up. |
| Asking more questions in meetings | 🟡 Ongoing | No clear opportunity yet but keeping it top of mind. The camera-off pattern works against this. |

## Areas for growth

**Needs independent ownership of something significant.** The extending eligibility/SKU trial rearchitect from last quarter remains his strongest recent achievement. Since then it's been a string of smaller tasks and scope-shrunk projects. The "get started" tasks for Q4 are the best current option, but not big enough to be a promotion-case anchor. Need to find or create a meatier opportunity.

**Speak up more in technical discussions.** The discussion post showed he has strong, well-formed opinions about team process and architecture. That energy doesn't show up in real-time meetings. FR sync facilitation for the next two weeks is a good forcing function.

**Proactive communication.** Still need to pull information out in 1:1s. The March 27 meeting was better (he brought up the discussion post, GHAS billing, autopilot), but the pattern of low-engagement 1:1s from February hasn't fully reversed.

**Channel the AI expertise into visible, team-impacting work.** The Autopilot tool, the Figma MCP exploration, the Copilot instructions files, the discussion post: these are all strong individual contributions. The next step is turning this into something with broader organizational visibility, like sharing Autopilot internally, presenting the agentic workflow ideas at a retro or broader forum, or driving adoption of Figma MCP across the team.

# Monthly check-in: 2026-02

_Chanakya Valluri, February 2026_
_FY26 H2_

## Summary

Mixed month. Chanakya was productive on bug fixes and investigation work, but the pattern of doing lots of small tasks without owning something meaty continues. He transitioned onto CSRA late in the month (Milestone 3) and immediately got blocked by Jennifer making updates to the same area. The billing budgets goal hasn't started yet. Job satisfaction dropped to 5/10 and he seemed low-energy in 1:1s, especially the Feb 25 one where he said he didn't have anything to talk about.

## Highlights

- Investigated the prod-sdc-01 latency SLO breach and posted a write-up. However, this was flagged as top priority at the Feb 2 FR sync and he got very little done on it during the rest of his FR shift.
- Resolved a batch of lingering FR issues using Copilot: API/schema mismatches, bot visibility filter causing job failures, GHAS trial seat display for Visual Studio customers. Good cleanup work.
- Started CSRA Milestone 3 work: added TriggerCodeScanningAssessmentJob for TWIRP integration.
- Adequate throughput on CSRA contributions. Not amazing, but not bad either.
- 19 PRs merged (22 opened, 3 closed), 19 reviewed, median PR age 23 hours.

## Concerns

- **Low engagement / low energy.** Feb 25 transcript: "I don't have anything to say today." Combined with 5/10 satisfaction and the general vibe of recent 1:1s, something's off. Could be personal (mom's hospital stuff came up Feb 4), could be work-related. Worth a direct conversation.
- **Still no ownership of a significant workstream.** Chanakya has been doing solid support/investigation/bug-fix work for months, but he hasn't DRI'd anything substantial. The billing budgets goal was supposed to change that, but it hasn't started. CSRA Milestone 3 is a step in the right direction but he got blocked almost immediately.
- **Code review volume is low (19).** For context, Nic did 38 and Jennifer did 45. This was called out in the review as a growth area ("increase code review participation") and it hasn't improved yet.
- **Passive communication style.** In 1:1s, Chanakya rarely brings topics or updates proactively. Most information has to be pulled out. This makes it hard to know what's actually going on with his work.

## Goal progress

| Goal | Status | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Ship hard billing budgets with thorough planning | 🔴 Not started | Staying on CSRA for 1-2 more iterations first. Target: end of April. Kickoff meeting with Sylvan and Lucy still needs scheduling. |

### Development areas (from H1 review)

- **Increase code review participation:** Not improved. 19 reviews this month vs team average of ~34. Needs to review PRs across all team epics, not just his own work.
- **Proactive communication:** Not improved. 1:1s still feel like pulling teeth. Chanakya needs to come prepared with updates, blockers, and questions.
- **Deeper investigation before asking for help:** Some positive signal here from the SLO deep-dive. He did solid independent investigation work on that one.

## GitHub activity

| Metric | Count |
|--------|-------|
| PRs merged | 19 |
| Median PR age (hours) | 23 |
| PRs reviewed | 19 |
| Issues created | 5 |
| Issues closed | 16 |

### All PRs

- [Add GHAS upsell to org-level security settings for enterprise admins](https://github.com/github/github/pull/416674) (merged)
- [Add GHAS upsell to org-level security settings for enterprise admins](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/12418) (merged)
- [Stafftools: Display trial type and show correct controls for active trials](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417146) (merged)
- [Fix enterprise coverage stats authorization to match security coverage page](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417284) (merged)
- [Simplify coverage page links to use consistent archived:false filter](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/12827) (merged)
- [Clean up enterprise security configurations on trial cancel/expire](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417287) (merged)
- [Accessibility issue requires fix in github/github-ui repository](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417289) (closed)
- [Fix flaky export controller tests with time-dependent date validation](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417291) (merged)
- [Fix schema inaccuracy: Add security_configuration_id to secret_scanning_delegated_bypass_options schema](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417294) (closed)
- [Fix schema/example mismatch for enterprise code security configurations API](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417295) (merged)
- [Fix schema/example mismatch for /orgs/{org}/security-managers endpoint](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417296) (merged)
- [Fix schema/example mismatch: Add security_configuration_id to bypass reviewer schema](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417297) (merged)
- [Fix accessibility: Add unique legendLabel to Highcharts config for landmark uniqueness](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/12833) (merged)
- [Fix flaky test: Use Date.current for consistent cooldown calculation](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417298) (merged)
- [Fix GHAS trial seat display for Visual Studio bundled license customers](https://github.com/github/github/pull/417323) (merged)
- [Deep-dive investigation: prod-sdc-01 latency-driven Security Center SLO breaches](https://github.com/github/copilot-sre/pull/2295) (closed)
- [Investigation: SecurityProductsEnablement job failures from bot visibility filter](https://github.com/github/copilot-sre/pull/2348) (closed)
- [Bypass ES search for visibility-only filters in OrganizationSecurityConfigurationJob](https://github.com/github/github/pull/418244) (merged)
- [Add TriggerCodeScanningAssessmentJob for CSRA TWIRP integration](https://github.com/github/github/pull/419435) (merged)
- [Move in-progress banner above the page header](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/14520) (merged)
- [Bump turboscan-client to latest](https://github.com/github/github/pull/420374) (merged)
- [Add ref to TriggerCodeScanningAssessmentJob TWIRP options](https://github.com/github/github/pull/420526) (merged)

## Coaching notes

### What's working

- Bug-fix throughput is consistently good. He finds and fixes real issues.
- Median PR age of 23 hours is fast, shows he's not letting things sit.
- Cleaned up a good batch of FR issues efficiently using Copilot.

### What needs to change

- **Needs a meaty project.** The billing budgets goal exists for a reason: Chanakya needs to prove he can own a significant workstream end-to-end with good planning and communication. The longer this stays at "not started," the more it looks like avoidance rather than sequencing.
- **Code reviews need to go up.** This is a concrete, measurable thing. He should be reviewing 30+ PRs/month, across the team's work, not just adjacent to his own tasks.
- **Come to 1:1s prepared.** Consider asking Chanakya to write 2-3 bullet points before each 1:1: what he worked on, what's blocking him, what he wants to discuss. The current pattern of "nothing to talk about" isn't sustainable.

### Suggested 1:1 discussion points

- "How are you feeling about work lately? You seemed low-energy the last couple weeks and I want to make sure everything's okay."
- "Let's put a concrete date on the billing budgets kickoff. What needs to happen before you're ready to start?"
- "I'd like you to start reviewing more PRs across the team, not just your own area. Can we set a target of 8+ per week?"

## Overall assessment

Chanakya is a reliable bug-fixer and investigator, but he's plateauing. The pattern of small tasks without significant ownership is a concern for his growth. The billing budgets goal is the right next step, and the sooner he starts it, the better. The low engagement signals need a direct conversation. He might need a reset: clear expectations, concrete milestones, and more structured 1:1s.

# Monthly Check-In: 2026-01

_Chanakya Valluri, January 2026_
_FY26 H2_

## Accomplishments

Took ownership over GHAS usage cards for the quick setup experience

Stafftools improvement - button for bundling/unbundling a configuration when there's a mismatch

What can we do to improve this?

## Opportunities

## GitHub Activity

| Metric | Count |
|--------|-------|
| PRs merged | 13 |
| Median PR age (hours) | 12 |
| PRs reviewed | 10 |
| Issues created | 7 |
| Issues closed | 6 |

# Monthly check-in: 2026-03
_Jennifer Ramirez, March 2026_

## Accomplishments

March was Jennifer's highest-output month on the team. She drove CSRA from mid-implementation through near-completion of MVP, closing out Milestones 1 through 4.

- **23 PRs merged** across github/github, github/github-ui, and github/airflow-sources. Full-stack: backend enablement controllers and routes ([bulk enablement service](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422147), [enablement repo picker endpoint](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422827), [email on completion](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423184), [pricing data endpoint](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423417)), frontend UI ([scan eligibility handling](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16806), [pricing dialog wiring](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16333), [repo picker polish](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15893), [scan state fixes](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15769)), and data pipeline work ([airflow table additions](https://github.com/github/airflow-sources/pull/48029)).
- **35 issues created, 50 closed.** Created all Milestone 4 sub-issues (enablement, notifications, pricing, repo picker refactors) and closed the bulk of Milestones 1-4 parent issues. This is DRI ownership at its best: scoping, tracking, and closing the loop.
- **34 PRs reviewed.** Sustained high review volume for the third consecutive month, unblocking Nic and Chanakya consistently across both repos.
- **Bug bash driven methodically.** Created the [Bug Bash Findings](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/issues/2640) tracking issue, worked through Catu's design feedback before creating follow-up issues, and resolved findings systematically (total repos tile bugs, scan state display, repo picker validation).
- **Proactively identified volume billing edge case.** Non-metered accounts needed pricing calculator data hidden while still allowing enablement. Volume billing wasn't originally considered until Jennifer raised it (feedback log, 2026-03-16).
- **Dorothy deferred CSRA scope decisions to Jennifer.** "Check with Jennifer on what was descoped" and "confirm billing models scope with Jennifer" (2026-03-17). Product trusts her as the technical and scope authority on CSRA.
- **Steve sought out Jennifer's process as a model.** He asked to pair with her specifically on how she writes and maintains issues for an epic, because CSRA was so well organized. He offered to record the walkthrough for others (2026-03-19).
- **Participated in SC3 interview loop.** Assigned Stack Undo (new 2026 interview format) for the March 31 interviews with two candidates.
- **Ashwin called out her performance explicitly.** Noted Jennifer is "performing like crazy" coming back from maternity leave, citing her as a contrast point for staff-level expectations (2026-03-12).

## Concerns

- **Pregnancy and leave planning.** Due September 29. Coverage planning for CSRA and ongoing ownership should begin in earnest. Not urgent yet, but the next few months are the window to build redundancy.
- **Post-CSRA assignment.** CSRE on GHAS is tentatively next (mentioned in Chanakya's 1:1). Need to confirm this is appropriately scoped and interesting enough to sustain momentum and growth trajectory. The Q4 roadmap overall is "not technically interesting" (Mike's assessment shared with Chanakya).
- **Coordination gap during parallel work.** Chanakya picked up a CSRA issue without syncing on Jennifer's parallel work, leading to a button wired to the wrong dialogue (~12 issues in flight, no sync before pickup). Jennifer flagged the confusion, which was the right instinct. Worth discussing clearer handoff norms now that multiple people are in the same codebase.
- **Patrick Knight (PM) leaving April 4.** Rohan filling in but already stretched. Two changelogs in flight. This could create product-side ambiguity on CSRA ship decisions and post-MVP prioritization.

## Goal progress

### Lead CSRA toward predictable, high-quality delivery
🟢 Strong. Milestones 1-4 closed. Bug bash executed and findings resolved. E2E testing issues embedded per milestone and closed. Stakeholder communication maintained through demos and syncs. This goal is effectively met pending final MVP ship.

### Increase impact outside the team
🟡 Mixed. CSRA naturally creates cross-team exposure (code scanning, Actions, billing, design). Steve modeling his issue management process after hers is a signal of influence. But no new broadly-visible artifacts (design docs, knowledge sharing sessions, channel posts) shipped this month. The focus was heads-down delivery, which is appropriate for a ship deadline. The real test is April onward.

### Improve written communication (why over what)
⏸️ Not assessed. No new ADRs or design docs this month. PR descriptions and issue content remain solid but this goal needs a new artifact to evaluate against.

### Staff eng vs management decision
Leaning staff engineer path (confirmed 2026-03-11). Interested in writing a sub-ADR for Steve's sequential enablement work, which would address both "impact outside team" and "written communication" goals simultaneously. Good instinct, worth encouraging.

### Advance consistent persistence of security product enablements
⏸️ Still on hold. Right call given CSRA timeline.

## Areas for growth

- **Self-directed problem identification.** After CSRA ships, does Jennifer find her next high-impact problem independently or wait for assignment? This is the single biggest differentiator between strong P4 and credible staff case. The CSRE on GHAS assignment is already on the roadmap, so the question is whether she also identifies adjacent work nobody asked for.
- **Mentorship visibility.** Jennifer has complimented Nic's work unprompted multiple times, and she's directing Chanakya and Nic on milestones effectively. But the shift from coordination (telling people what to work on) to mentorship (developing their independent judgment) needs to be more visible. If it's already happening, she should make it trackable.
- **Outward-facing artifacts.** The trials discussion post (December) was the last broadly-visible artifact. The sub-ADR for Steve's sequential enablement work would be a strong next step. Running a knowledge sharing session on CSRA architecture for the broader org would be another concrete move.
- **Written depth in design artifacts.** The H1 and calibration feedback about "why over what" in ADRs and PR descriptions hasn't had a new data point this cycle. Next ADR or design doc is the opportunity to demonstrate improvement.

# Monthly Check-In: 2026-02

_Jennifer Ramirez, February 2026_
_FY26 H2_

## Summary

February was a strong month. Jennifer continued as DRI on CSRA, pushing from early scaffolding through Milestone 1 completion and into Milestone 2. She authored 10 merged PRs across three repos, created 21 issues, and reviewed 45 PRs. Job satisfaction hit 10/10 for the first time.

The big themes this month: sustained delivery, proactive stakeholder management under pressure, and increasingly visible team coordination. She also shared that she is 9 weeks pregnant, due September 29.

At the P4 level, Jennifer is performing very well. The question this check-in tries to answer: what would it take to make a credible staff case? The gaps are real but closeable.

## Highlights & wins

- **Strong CSRA delivery velocity.** 10 PRs merged spanning backend controllers, API endpoints, audit logging, frontend repo picker, and analytics instrumentation. Full-stack work across github/github, github/github-ui, and github/audit-log-allowlists. Milestone 1 reached near-completion by Feb 18, with Milestone 2 already 4/7 issues in progress.
- **Proactive stakeholder management under ambiguity.** When Sales Security Specialists raised late concerns about CSRA/SRA coupling and monorepo failures, Jennifer came prepared with technical data (P99 code scanning runtime, 1-hour timeout, per-repo graceful failure handling) and proposed pragmatic alternatives (click analytics instead of an A/B test). Good substantive engagement, not just pushback. Strong DRI behavior.
- **Active milestone planning and team coordination.** Mapped out Milestones 1-5 with clear scope boundaries and ownership. Directed Nic to splitting rework, moved Chanakya to Milestone 3, created E2E testing issues within each milestone. This is leading without formal authority, and doing it well.
- **Excellent stakeholder communication on CSRA.** Proactively set up weekly syncs with Dorothy and Catu and consistently shared demo videos throughout the project. This kept stakeholders confident in progress and eliminated surprises.
- **Unprompted praise from Dorothy.** Consistent positive signal from the PM side on Jennifer's CSRA ownership.
- **High code review volume.** 45 PRs reviewed, roughly 2+ per working day. This is carrying the team's review load and unblocking others, which is valuable even if it comes at the cost of her own code output.
- **Job satisfaction at all-time high (10/10).** Driven by the nature of growth work: new but in a familiar domain.

## Concerns & areas to watch

- **Pregnancy and leave planning.** Due September 29. Coverage planning for CSRA and ongoing ownership should start soon. This is not urgent but should not wait until Q3.

## Goal progress

### Active goals

- **Communicate Unified GHAS Trial Progress:** ✅ Complete. Co-authored with Dorothy, published Dec 18 as [Discussion #2347](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/discussions/2347). 41% drop in support tickets, eligible self-serve customers from 350 to >32k.
- **Lead CSRA Toward Predictable, High-Quality Delivery:** 🟢 On track. Milestones 1-5 scoped with clear ownership. E2E testing embedded per milestone. Consistent demos to stakeholders.
- **Advance Consistent Persistence of Security Product Enablements:** ⏸️ On hold. No movement this month, and that's the right call. Jennifer has been fully focused on CSRA as DRI of a high-priority epic with a March 31 deadline. This goal makes sense to revisit after CSRA ships.

### Development goals

- **Increase Impact Outside the Team:** Mixed. CSRA provides cross-team exposure, and the trials post (completed in December) was a solid artifact. But this month the focus was heads-down delivery, which is appropriate for the timeline. The question is whether Jennifer will sustain the outward-facing work after CSRA ships.
- **Improve Written Communication (why over what):** Not assessed this month. No new ADRs or design docs to evaluate.
- **Staff Eng vs Management Decision:** No update. Expected by mid-2026.

## Action item status

- ✅ **Milestone-by-milestone timeline for CSRA:** Milestones 1-5 scoped and planned as of Feb 25.
- ✅ **Click analytics for start/cancel scan buttons:** Shipped in github-ui #14023.
- ✅ **Add milestone timeline to weekly status updates:** Confirmed done. Jennifer has been including milestone timelines consistently.
- 🟡 **Dorothy review of E2E testing plan:** Planned but not confirmed complete.
- ➡️ **Subscription trial dead code cleanup:** Chanakya owns this. Not Jennifer's task.

## Coaching notes

### What to reinforce

- **DRI ownership is excellent.** Jennifer is shaping technical direction, pushing back on stakeholders when warranted, proposing alternatives, and coordinating teammates. This is exactly what strong P4 looks like.
- **Stakeholder communication has leveled up.** The weekly syncs with Dorothy, the demo videos, the proactive scope management: this is noticeably better than earlier in the cycle when the feedback was "add milestone dates to status updates."

### What staff level looks like (and what's missing)

Based on the H1 review, the recurring feedback, and the P4 job expectations, here's what Jennifer would need to demonstrate for a credible staff case:

1. **Identify and drive work that isn't on anyone's roadmap.** The end-of-trial reset unification was a great example. The trials post was another. But these are still infrequent. At staff level, this should be a consistent pattern, not occasional highlights. After CSRA ships, watch whether Jennifer finds her next high-impact problem or waits for assignment.

2. **Produce design artifacts and sustain outward-facing impact.** Jennifer has not written a broadly-visible design doc, RFC, or architectural proposal this cycle. The CSRA ADR exists but was noted in the H1 review as needing more "why." Staff engineers typically produce documents that influence technical decisions across teams, and share knowledge at a steady rhythm: running sessions, writing docs, being sought out as a domain expert by people outside the team. Jennifer has the domain knowledge to do this (she's basically the org's trials expert). The trials post was a good artifact but a one-time event co-authored with a PM. The question is whether she'll invest the time to make this a consistent pattern. This is the biggest gap.

3. **Turn coordination into mentorship.** Jennifer is directing Nic and Chanakya effectively (assigning milestones, sequencing work). The next level is developing their independent judgment: helping them make architectural decisions on their own, coaching them through ambiguous problems, not just telling them what to work on next. Note: this mentorship may already be happening and just isn't visible to me. If so, the action is for Jennifer to make it more visible or track it consistently so the impact can be recognized.

4. **Written communication depth.** The H1 review specifically called out descriptive vs reflective writing. ADRs should include trade-off analysis and rationale for rejected alternatives. PR descriptions should explain why, not just what. This is both a skill gap and a visibility gap: deeper writing creates artifacts that travel beyond the team.

### Suggested 1:1 discussion points

- "After CSRA ships, what's the highest-impact problem you could work on that nobody has asked you to solve?" (Tests for self-directed impact identification)
- "How are you developing Nic and Chanakya's ability to make technical decisions independently?" (Shifts from coordination to mentorship framing)
- "Would you be interested in running a knowledge sharing session on CSRA architecture for the broader org?" (Concrete next step for outward visibility)

## Overall assessment

Jennifer is performing very well at P4. She is driving a complex, multi-milestone, cross-team project with minimal supervision; managing scope and stakeholder concerns proactively; producing full-stack contributions across three repos; and coordinating two teammates without formal authority. The 45 PR reviews shows she's also unblocking the team at a high rate.

For staff consideration, the evidence is not yet there. The main gaps are: limited design artifacts visible beyond the team, coordination that hasn't yet shifted into visible mentorship, and outward-facing impact that is still occasional rather than habitual. These are closeable gaps, and CSRA has positioned Jennifer well to close them. The post-CSRA period (April onward) will be the real test: does she find her next high-impact problem independently, or does she wait to be assigned?

February was a strong month. The trajectory is positive.

# Monthly check-in: 2026-03
_Nic Daantos, March 2026_

## Accomplishments

**CSRA delivery continued at high volume.** 13 PRs merged across github/github and github-ui. Shipped the [enablement banner](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15996), [generalized the SRA pricing calculator](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15884) for reuse in CSRA, built the [CSRA committer counts endpoint](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422470), added [assessment failure and no-vulnerabilities result states](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/15535), and delivered multiple rounds of repo picker and dialog polish. Full-stack work spanning backend endpoints, frontend components, and data filtering logic.

**Second FR rotation showed real growth.** Self-assessed that he now has "a fighting chance" reading escalations and customer issues, compared to having no idea during his first rotation. Shipped the [mismatched configs transition](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423425) using a solid hybrid approach (primaries for direct writes, wrapped downstream calls in write-to pattern). Handled the Wayfair incident transition to [re-enable Code Security](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/issues/2691) same-day. Investigated 422 causes on security config save for several hours.

**Incident #4131 response (March 24).** Participated in the incident call where Adobe's mass security config apply overwhelmed the collab MySQL primary. Part of the team that traced root cause from 500 errors to the specific query and job within ~20 minutes. Three repair items shipped.

**Handled an operational mistake with maturity.** Accidentally ran the SS mismatch config transition instead of the Wayfair transition, hitting ~9% of mismatched repos before catching and cancelling it. Rather than hiding it, he immediately surfaced it with full context: what happened, that it was careless, that the end state was correct, and a heads-up to watch for customer escalations. Then wrote and ran the correct Wayfair transition the same day. Got kudos from cardigancat and Mike for the fast recovery. Credited the team culture for feeling safe enough to be transparent, contrasting it with how he would have panicked at a previous employer.

**39 PRs reviewed.** Highest review count on the team this month. Engaged substantively on complex work: transition PRs for extended metadata backfills ([customs configs](https://github.com/github/github/pull/416256), [GHR repos](https://github.com/github/github/pull/416244), [repos attached to custom configs](https://github.com/github/github/pull/416265)), Steve's [attachment pipeline ADR](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/pull/2679), Quick Setup PRs, and CSRA work. Left a thoughtful review comment on the [landing page FAQ PR](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16790) asking whether a shared component would be better than per-page duplication.

**Promo calibration completed.** Calibrated at 140 with no pushback from leadership. Jessica called it "a slam dunk to the slam-dunked power." Promo packet is with Ashwin, discussions mid-May.

## Concerns

**Issue creation is still low.** 2 issues created this month (up from 0 in February). Both were reactive: one for the Wayfair transition, one for the EstimateCostDialog rename. At SE2, he should be identifying gaps and filing issues proactively. This is the most persistent gap in his profile.

**FR visibility needs improvement.** Mike flagged that Nic's daily Geekbot standup updates during FR just say "first responder" with no detail on what issues came in, what he investigated, or what's pending. The team and manager should be able to see the work happening without asking. Nic needs to be noisier in team chat about active investigations.

**Investigation skills still developing.** Nic self-identified this as a growth area. He acknowledged being slower on the 422 investigation and having difficulty piecing together information. The accidental transition run also points to needing more careful verification steps before executing production operations.

**Two-week absence starting March 30.** Out until April 15 during a period where CSRA is wrapping up and billing budgets is queued up for him. Handoff was discussed in the March 27 1:1.

## Goal progress

| Goal | Status | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Lead a medium-sized, customer-facing project end-to-end | 🟢 On track | CSRA ownership strong across both milestones. Billing budgets assigned as next DRI project. |
| Become an independent FR contributor | 🟢 On track | Second rotation showed clear improvement. Handled mismatched configs transition, Wayfair incident recovery, incident response. Still needs to build investigation speed. |
| Proactively improve code quality and system integrity | 🟡 In progress | [Replica DB reads PR](https://github.com/github/github/pull/424706), EstimateCostDialog rename, and the conditional bug fix for mismatch checks are good signals. Issue creation still lagging. |

## Areas for growth

**Be noisier during FR.** Share what's happening in standup updates and team chat. "First responder" as a standup update tells nobody anything. List the issues that came in, what you investigated, what's still open.

**Create issues proactively.** When you notice a gap, an edge case, or something that could be improved, file an issue. Two issues in a month of active FR and development work is too few for someone heading toward SE2. The EstimateCostDialog rename was a good example of spotting something and acting on it. Do more of that.

**Engage in design and ADR discussions.** The FAQ PR review comment (suggesting a shared component) was a good example. Do this more often, especially on CSRA ADRs and architectural decisions. # March 2026 check-in – Steve Richert 

## What you shipped 

Attachment pipeline ADR. Shipped! Aakash approved it, and you incorporated feedback from Tara and Aakash across two rounds. This is the most significant written artifact you've produced in months. 

Uptime SLO fix. The index hints PR ([#422153](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422153)) merged and the main latency monitor is green thanks to your solid diagnostic work tracing the root cause down to Hydro delivery issues. Good pushback on Javier's suggestion to filter to first delivery attempts only, even though we decided to move on from it in the end. 

Priority prediction challenge. You wrote a structured [proposal](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/discussions/2643) for an internal challenge to identify good candidates for security feature enablement, and started building it. Great example of thinking proactively to identify your own impact. 

Knowledge sharing with Jennifer. You ran a session on creating issues and batches with Copilot and wrote up the [discussion post](https://github.com/github/security-products-enablement/discussions/2685). Still hasn't been shared broadly and that would still be great to do! 

## GitHub activity 

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Metric | Count | Trend vs. Feb |
| PRs merged | 4   | ⬆️ (from 1) |
| PRs reviewed | 12  | ⬆️ (from 1) |

PRs merged: stale config cleanup ([#421262](https://github.com/github/github/pull/421262)), index hints ([#422153](https://github.com/github/github/pull/422153)), Docker build fix ([spe-priority-prediction#5](https://github.com/github/spe-priority-prediction/pull/5)), moda config fix ([spe-priority-prediction#7](https://github.com/github/spe-priority-prediction/pull/7)). 

Code reviews went from 1 to 12. That's progress, and a few of them were very impactful, but it missed the 25/month target from last check-in. For context, the team shipped over 100 PRs again in March. Reviewing 12 of them is not staff-level engagement with the team's output. 

## Where to focus in April 

Ship earlier artifacts. We talked about the fat marker sketch in March: a rough, intentionally incomplete architecture doc that shows direction before the full ADR exists. That's a great idea, and we need to follow that theme with all your work. You should never be working on something for multiple weeks without anything to show for it. This also enables... 

Make the work visible. Very few people outside of our team have seen the ADR, the prediction challenge, the knowledge sharing session, the SLO fix, etc. Post in the security products channel. Present the ADR at the org-wide review meeting. Loop in Aaron and Ashwin proactively. This has been a consistent theme in your feedback for over a year now. 

Drive the attachment pipeline forward. This is the best staff-level opportunity on the roadmap. The vertical slices need to be created, and coordinating with Chanakya's process proposal is great, but we don’t want to delay getting started any further. It's been months since we talked about adding this to Q3 - it's Q4 now, and we need momentum and impact. 

## Overall 

March was better than February, but your impact is still not at staff level. The ADR took months to finish. The SO uptime SLO was a WIP for over a month. 12 code reviews missed the target by more than half. 4 PRs is low without a significant amount of non-coding impact/artifacts. And the work you delivered wasn't visible or impactful outside of our team. 

We've been in a pattern where direct feedback leads to a burst of improvement, then things slide back. Staff engineers at GitHub are expected to drive visible, self-directed impact across teams. That means finding your own high-impact problems, producing artifacts that reach beyond the immediate team, and doing it consistently, not in bursts after feedback. 

And to be clear, the quality of your work when you're engaged isn't the problem! It's consistency, volume, and visibility we need to focus on.

# Performance check-in: February 2026

## Summary

February was a lost month in terms of visible impact: 1 small PR merged, 1 PR reviewed, 0 issues created, no notable docs/writeups shared. I know you spent significant time on Sequential Enablement research and the SO uptime SLO investigation, but neither produced visible or shippable artifacts. Research/investigation needs to turn into visible output faster.

## Highlights

- Convinced the CSRA team to remove the date picker for historical runs, which eliminated some unnecessary complexity
- Discovered the Security Center concurrent sliced query pattern issue was a legit problem instead of assuming it was just low traffic SLO flapping.
- Sequential Enablement ADR progressing via a grumpy and combative Copilot partner - love to see creative use of AI like that.

## What I need to see change

**Visible output in general needs to go up.** February had almost no tangible impact. I get that staff-level work sometimes means less code and more architecture, mentorship, and influence, but research and thinking need to turn into artifacts faster. 

**The SO uptime SLO issue needs to close out.** It has been the top priority since the start of your FR shift almost four weeks ago and it's still ongoing. We need consistent daily progress on it until it's resolved. 

**The Sequential Enablement ADR needs to be published.** It's been in progress since at least January. I love the adversarial Copilot approach and I'm glad you're finding value in AI to harden the plan, but it's dragging. What's a realistic deadline for a reviewable draft?

**Code reviews.** You're the most senior engineer on the team and your code reviews are some of the most valuable feedback anyone gets. 1 review in a month means the team isn't getting that benefit. The team shipped over 100 PRs last month, and it's increasing month over month. I'd like you to review at least 1/5 of them, which means a target of 5+ per week.

**Calendar and Slack.** Missing multiple meetings because notifications weren't set up after the M365 switch shouldn't happen, and the Slack responsiveness feedback from your H1 review is still relevant.

## Goal progress from H1 review

Your H1 review goals were:

> - Successfully support the GHAS rollout for large enterprises, including Apple. Deliver all required GHES backports, validate safety at scale, and reduce related escalation volume by at least 50 percent by the end of the period.
> - Lead a cross-team security feature from design to ship. Drive a security-focused project (pricing, enablement, or detection-related) from initial problem framing to production launch, with clear success metrics and customer impact.
> - Improve the reliability and observability of the billing and enablement systems. Reduce false-positive billing states and improve signal-to-noise in related alerts, aiming for at least a 30 percent reduction in manual debugging time for partner teams.
> - Strengthen my technical influence within the organization. Share at least two design docs or architectural proposals that help clarify confusing areas of the monolith or unify patterns across security features.
> - Grow my leadership presence by mentoring and unblocking others. Provide consistent support to at least two engineers on adjacent teams, helping them ramp up on security systems, billing logic, or monolith patterns.

I know Copilot wrote them but let's check in on them...

| Goal | Status | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Support GHAS rollout for large enterprises (Apple) | ⏸️ Paused | Pushed the final patch early Feb, holding pattern since then |
| Lead a cross-team security feature from design to ship | 🔴 Behind | Sequential Enablement ADR still in draft, no other clear opportunities |
| Improve reliability/observability of billing and enablement systems | 🟢 Complete | The stale data cleanup job has been a great success |
| Strengthen technical influence (design docs/proposals) | 🔴 Behind | No completed design docs or proposals so far this cycle |
| Mentoring and unblocking others | 🟡 Mixed | CSRA consulting was/is valuable but code review volume was very low |

# Monthly check-in: 2026-03

_Tara Kintner, March 2026_
## Accomplishments

**Quick Setup shipped!** The MVP shipped on March 17 after months of Tara leading the effort end-to-end. The second bug bash found almost nothing, which reflects the quality bar Tara held throughout.

**Extended Quick Setup to bundled metered.** Immediately after MVP ship, Tara added support for bundled metered customers ([github-ui#16497](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16497), [github#423827](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423827)). The PRs were ready to ship while Tara went on PTO so that momentum could continue without them around. Also added trial info to the security configurations payload ([github#423771](https://github.com/github/github/pull/423771)), cost text for Quick Setup ([github-ui#16355](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/16355)), and ELC support ([github-ui#14670](https://github.com/github/github-ui/pull/14670)).

**SLOs and monitoring.** Added Quick Setup controllers to SLOs ([datadog-monitoring#46937](https://github.com/github/datadog-monitoring/pull/46937)). Discovered that existing SLOs had a stamp key filter only matching com, WU, and WUS, potentially masking availability issues for roughly two years 😱. 

Also proactively audited monitoring coverage across controllers, created a Datadog monitor for 422s, and proposed a plan for determining which controllers belong in SLOs (user-story-driven) vs. operational monitors, then systematically adding coverage.

**Mentoring and knowledge sharing.** Walked Catu through using the Copilot agent button in the github-ui repo, teaching her how to create agent-driven PRs and preview deploys. She has since shipped a few things and has talked multiple times about how she loves having this new power. 

## Goal progress

**Quick Setup delivery (H2 goal): complete.** MVP shipped March 17. Extended to bundled metered. Next phase is volume support, which Tara will own starting April 3.

**Written and shared knowledge (reflection goal): incremental progress.** Sharing Corner continues. Taught Catu Copilot workflows. Used Copilot for architectural documentation. No standalone written artifact published this month, though. This goal still needs a concrete deliverable.

**Observability and reliability (emerging goal): strong progress.** The SLO audit, monitoring coverage framework, 422 monitoring, and stamp filter discovery all demonstrate Tara thinking systemically about reliability. This is exactly the kind of P4 work that raises the bar for the whole team. 

**Copilot adoption (personal goal): sustained.** Continues to use Copilot effectively for tedious template work, architectural docs, and as a pair programmer. Finding their own workflow rather than following the hype.

## Areas for growth

It's all just stuff we've talked about 10 times. Written knowledge sharing, cross org visibility, side quests on interesting and generally valuable self-identified problems. No need to rehash it all. These will continue to be the gap blocking Staff-level consideration, which is fine as long as Tara continues to be OK with it.
